headline: ZSE effects new indexing
content: CBZ added to Top 15 Index, Afdis falls 5% to $3","The Zimbabwe Stock Exchange effected new indexing on January 1, 2020 in line with the adoption of Global Industry Classification Standards, reclassifying all listed counters into financials, consumer discretionary, consumer staples, materials, industrials, information communication and technology, and real estate indices with five new index constituencies designed to provide better tools for performance measurement and sector-based investment strategies, as the equities market opened the year on positive momentum with the All Share Index rising 0.06 percent to 233.67 points, Top 10 Index gaining 0.38 percent to 208.63 points, Top 15 Index adding 0.31 percent to 209.6 points, and Small Cap Index pacing fastest at 0.38 percent to 636.33 points, while the Medium Cap Index eased 0.67 percent to 283.67 points. Under the new classification, CBZ Holdings joins Meikles, Nampak, National Foods and Seed Co Limited to complete the Top 15 Index alongside Top 10 constituents BAT, Cassava, Delta, Econet, Innscor, OK Zimbabwe, Old Mutual, Padenga, Seed Co International and Simbisa, while the Small Cap Index comprises Art, Border Timbers, Cafca, CFI, Cottco, Edgars, Falgold, Fidelity, General Beltings, Hwange, Masimba, Medtech, NTS, Powerspeed, Truworths, Turnall, Unifreight, Willdale, Zeco, Zimpapers and ZPI. On the first trading day under the new framework, Willdale headlined gainers with 15 percent to 3.47c, TSL rose 5 percent to 80c, OK and Zimplow gained 4 percent each, and ZB added 2 percent, while African Distillers Limited fell 5 percent to $3 alongside RioZim (-16% to $2.25), Edgars (-14% to 18c), National Foods (-0.11% to $9) and Delta (-0.08% to $3.45), with turnover at $6.7 million and total market capitalisation at $30.2 billion.

headline: ZSE effects new indexing
content: CBZ added to Top 15 Index, Afdis falls 5% to $3","The Zimbabwe Stock Exchange effected new indexing on January 1, 2020 in line with the adoption of Global Industry Classification Standards, reclassifying all listed counters into financials, consumer discretionary, consumer staples, materials, industrials, information communication and technology, and real estate indices with five new index constituencies designed to provide better tools for performance measurement and sector-based investment strategies, as the equities market opened the year on positive momentum with the All Share Index rising 0.06 percent to 233.67 points, Top 10 Index gaining 0.38 percent to 208.63 points, Top 15 Index adding 0.31 percent to 209.6 points, and Small Cap Index pacing fastest at 0.38 percent to 636.33 points, while the Medium Cap Index eased 0.67 percent to 283.67 points. headline: Simbisa, OK change Karoi’s complexion
content: Simbisa Brands (Chicken Inn, Pizza Inn) and OK Zimbabwe opened outlets in Karoi, transforming the town's retail landscape. Simbisa opened late 2019, with OK Zimbabwe set to open weeks later. The investments created local employment, enhanced consumer choice, and positioned Karoi as a key stopover on the Harare-Chirundu Highway. Local authorities confirmed the development attracted interest from other investors, including tobacco companies and universities. Karoi's economy centers on agriculture (tobacco, maize, horticulture), tourism, and gold mining, providing a diverse customer base. A local business noted the new entrants would enforce pricing discipline on existing retailers. Residents expressed confidence in the town's growth trajectory and improved service diversity. For Simbisa, the outlet adds to its 459-store footprint across 9 countries (as of June 2019). For OK Zimbabwe, the store expands its nationwide presence and follows its FY2020 refurbishment program."

headline: OK volumes drop 15pc
content: OK Zimbabwe recorded a 15 percent drop in sales volumes for the third quarter ended December 31, 2019, an improvement from the 23 percent decline reported for the six months ended September 30, 2019, though cumulative nine-month volumes remained negative at 20 percent below prior year. Company secretary Margaret Munyuru stated in a trading update that the unstable and challenging business environment was characterised by currency depreciation, erratic power supplies, product supply challenges and significant increases in operating costs, with generator use for operations increasing costs significantly due to high fuel usage, servicing and repairs. Despite relative exchange rate stability during the quarter (October-December 2019), product prices continued to increase albeit at a slower pace. Month-on-month inflation dropped from 17.5 percent in November to 16.5 percent in December 2019 and 2.2 percent in January 2020. Stores were adequately stocked for the festive season, but demand was subdued reflecting the impact of continual erosion of incomes due to hyperinflation. Revenue for the quarter was up 10 percent in inflation-adjusted terms on prior year, with profit margins consistent with half-year performance. The group will maintain adequate stocks, pursue cost-containment measures, and continue investing in refurbishment of old stores and opening new ones in viable locations where not represented. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: Seedco downbeat over volume decline
content: Seed Co Limited reported depressed sales volumes for the half year ended September 30, 2019 with volumes down 46 percent on the back of non-recurrence of early maize seed sales made in the comparative period, while for the third quarter and nine months ended December 31, 2019 volumes fell 3 percent and 24 percent respectively compared to the same period in the prior year, according to a trading update. The seed manufacturing giant stated the outlook remains highly unpredictable due to the harsh economic environment characterized by inflationary pressures, waning disposable incomes, unfavourable weather forecasts, reduced Government uptake of seed under the Presidential Input Scheme and Command Agriculture, fuel shortages that hampered land preparations, and erratic electricity and water supplies that incapacitated wheat farmers from growing the crop under irrigation. Despite the volume decline, the group swung back to the black posting $34.4 million profit for the half year from a $29.1 million loss in the same period last year. Construction of a state-of-the-art artificial maize seed drying plant at the Stapleford complex is progressing with equipment already delivered from Denmark, although progress is slower than originally anticipated due to funding challenges. The company stated it will bank on its regional associate investment, Seed Co International Limited for real capital preservation and earnings. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: Zim economy: Are we clouded in our judgment?
content: CBZ Holdings chairman Marc Holtzman, five months into his tenure, granted one of his first interviews to local media outlining his vision for Zimbabwe's largest banking group with 23 percent market share, 221,300 bank accounts, 13 percent deposits market share and total assets worth $5.8 billion as at September 2019. Holtzman declared Zimbabwe 'one of the greatest investment opportunities I have ever seen' and expressed confidence by planning to bring his children for a visit, paralleling his investment in Rwanda's BK Group PLC where he serves as chairman and purchased 706,000 shares for his four minor children as 'expression of confidence in the bank's recent strong performance, growth prospects and leadership.' The bank extended $3 billion worth of loans to the agriculture sector under Government's Smart Agriculture Programme, mostly following his appointment, and is embarking on a massive restructuring exercise that has seen six executives depart and diaspora talent recruited including Tawanda Gumbo (16 years Deloitte Southern/West/Central Africa executive) as chief finance officer, Nyasha Mutsai from Standard Bank to lead talent management, and two executives from Goldman Sachs and Renaissance Capital. CBZ is launching CBZ Capital, positioned to become 'the premier corporate finance investment banking institution' in Zimbabwe. The article highlights CBZ's historical strategic role including facilitating fuel supply agreements between Zimbabwe, Kuwait and Libya in 2001 with then-managing director Dr Gideon Gono negotiating deals that saw Libyan Arab Foreign Bank provide $360 million for fuel supplies, and providing the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe with two governors—Dr John Mangudya (incumbent) and Dr Gideon Gono (predecessor). Holtzman's parting advice to investors was to 'go long and invest generously in Zimbabwe Inc.

headline: EcoCash increases transaction limits
content: "EcoCash increased daily and monthly transaction limits effective March 2020, with RBZ approval, to align with rising prices and customer needs [citation:article]. Key changes: P2P send limit from $1,000 to $5,000; merchant/bill payments from $3,000/$2,000 to $10,000; daily limits increased to $5,000 (non-linked), $10,000 (debit card linked), $25,000 (bank linked), and new 'Executive Grade' at $30,000; monthly limits raised to $20,000 (non-linked), $35,000 (debit card), $150,000 (bank), and $250,000 (Executive) [citation:1]. Econet's Fungayi Mandiveyi confirmed limits are regulated by RBZ and reviewed periodically. However, this was followed by severe regulatory restrictions from April 2020: daily limit slashed to $5,000, agent lines abolished, multiple wallets banned [citation:3][citation:7][citation:9]. By August 2020, EcoCash's share of National Payments System fell from 30% to 19%, with transaction volumes dropping below 800 million [citation:5][citation:9]. The incident highlights regulatory risk for mobile money operators."

headline: Dairibord Schools Rugby Festival postponed
content: Dairibord Zimbabwe, in partnership with Prince Edward School, postponed the 7th edition of the Dairibord Schools Rugby Festival (scheduled April 27-May 2, 2020) due to COVID-19. The statement emphasized health and safety as 'of utmost importance.' The festival had grown into one of the world's largest schools rugby tournaments, attracting international teams, referees, and scouts, and producing stars like Tendai Mtawarira (World Cup winner). The postponement demonstrated responsible corporate citizenship and alignment with Government's national disaster declaration. Subsequent rescheduling saw the festival successfully return in 2021 and 2022. Dairibord's sponsorship, running for over a decade, represents long-term commitment to grassroots sport development, talent identification, and brand building among youth and families. The festival serves as a critical platform for Dairibord's products (milks, yogurts, juices) to reach health-conscious consumers, while building brand affinity through association with positive youth development. The decision to prioritize safety over short-term marketing exposure reinforced brand trust and stakeholder relationships.

headline: JUST IN: Econet not closing
content: Econet Wireless Zimbabwe issued a statement distancing itself from false social media claims that the company was shutting down operations due to COVID-19. A spokesman clarified: 'There is no truth whatsoever in claims that we have declared a national shutdown. Our shops will be open tomorrow (Monday) and Econet will continue to offer its services to the public, albeit in a manner that is safe for our staff and customers.' The company emphasized that public health pronouncements are the prerogative of Government and healthcare experts. Econet had been actively collaborating with Ministry of Health, City of Harare, and other partners on COVID-19 public awareness campaigns. The swift response demonstrated crisis management capability and commitment to maintaining essential services. Subsequently, Econet accelerated digital transformation, introduced free health hotlines, and donated PPE, later receiving the COVID-19 Business Hero Award (African Leadership Magazine).

headline: CBZ appoints new board of directors
content: CBZ Holdings has announced its new board of directors chaired by veteran strategist and economist Marc Holtzman, with the mandate to drive the financial services giant's growth and transformation strategy, consolidate the group's position as a premier corporate finance and investment banking institution in Zimbabwe, and serve as a driver for change across Zimbabwe's financial services sector. The board includes group chief executive officer Dr Blessing Mudavanhu and Tawanda Gumbo as executive directors, and independent non-executive directors Rebecca Gaskin Gain, Edward Mashingaidze, Edward Galante and Louis Gerken. Holtzman stated the announcement is a major milestone laying the foundation for future success, with a key component being recruiting the best talent at the most senior level, and expressed confidence that under the new board's leadership CBZ is in the best possible position to deliver its strategic vision. The board is tasked with introducing a raft of positive changes including maintaining and growing dominant market share, integrating businesses, promoting innovation, enhancing digital services to improve customer experience while remaining competitive on pricing, enhancing shareholder value, ensuring customer satisfaction, and making significant contribution to the country's financial services sector. Holtzman noted that CBZ Holdings' market capitalisation is but a fraction of its true value, and the new board and management will tirelessly work to best serve customers and increase shareholder value, with the principal mission to financially transform lives and serve the people of Zimbabwe, including positioning CBZ as the gateway for foreign investment in Zimbabwe to support economic growth, boost human capital and create opportunities for global partnerships. The announcement includes a detailed corporate history tracing CBZ's establishment in 1980 as Bank of Credit and Commerce Zimbabwe Limited, Government takeover in 1991, privatisation and ZSE listing in 1998, establishment of CBZ Holdings in 2005, acquisition of Beverly Building Society in 2007, and 2010 operational consolidation integrating banking and building society services under one roof

headline: Dairibord posts 100 percent surge in forex earnings
content: Dairiboard Zimbabwe Limited recorded a 100 percent surge in foreign currency earnings to US$3.4 million for the year ended December 31, 2019 from US$1.7 million in the prior year, driven by continued implementation of a robust export strategy to increase regional footprint and generate foreign currency to cover import requirements. Turnover improved 60 percent to $1.115 billion driven by export growth and necessary product price adjustments, while sales volumes dropped 17 percent against an industry average of 23 percent for the manufacturing sector. Liquid milk sub-sector achieved marginal growth of 0.2 percent due to increase in local raw milk intake of 10 percent compared to national milk growth of 7.2 percent, benefiting from milk supply development initiatives targeted at small, medium and large scale milk producers. Beverages category declined 23 percent, food sub-sector declined 39 percent, with the drop in volumes minimised relative to industry performance due to strong brands and extensive market research, allowing the business to sustain capacity utilisation of 44 percent against manufacturing industry average of 36.4 percent. Operating profit of $90 million was achieved compared to $55 million in 2018, with operating profit margin of 8.1 percent attained up from 7.9 percent in prior year on account of reduced fixed overheads, diligent procurement practices, and intensified drive to improve operational efficiencies. Profit after tax increased to $142 million from $36 million in prior year. Foreign liabilities reduced 76 percent from US$3.9 million in 2018 to US$0.93 million in 2019 as the company focused on managing and reducing foreign currency denominated debts. Disposal of Dairiboard Malawi was finalised in Q3 2019, realising a gain of $15.2 million. The COVID-19 global pandemic is expected to have far reaching and unpredictable impact on doing business in 2020 and beyond, with very significant negative impact on the company's performance envisaged. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, Seed Co, Hippo Valley Estates, BAT Zimbabwe, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned

headline: Delta, Croco Motors chip in with more donations
content: Delta Corporation chairman Mr Canaan Dube presented a $5 million cheque to President Mnangagwa at State House on April 6, 2020 as part of the private sector's response to the COVID-19 pandemic, with an additional $1 million worth of goods committed for delivery the following day, while Croco Motors donated an ambulance worth US$90,000 to the COVID-19 Disaster Fund. President Mnangagwa stated the donations would capacitate Government to procure Personal Protective Equipment for frontline health workers, emphasizing: 'They need to be protected. We know what has happened elsewhere. We don't want to make the same mistake. We will have to do everything possible to protect our frontline soldiers.' The President acknowledged that the donations followed an appeal made to the business sector four days earlier, and commended local companies for diverting production lines to produce masks, gloves, sanitisers, gowns and caps domestically, with science universities now producing most of these materials. President Mnangagwa noted that the 21-day national lockdown decision was not taken lightly due to its impact on informal sector livelihoods, but was necessary to limit the spread of the pandemic, with social security networks expanded to take care of those affected, and the lockdown potentially extended depending on the situation. He also met with religious leaders from Jewish, Greek, Hellenic and Hindu communities who pledged assistance. This substantial corporate social responsibility commitment follows Delta's extensive humanitarian portfolio including RTGS$3.1 million in Cyclone Idai relief (DLTA022, DLTA026) and sustained sponsorship of sports and cultural events (DLTA013, DLTA019, DLTA020, DLTA024, DLTA027)

headline: Reduce prices by 20 percent: Econet
content: Zimbabwe's largest mobile phone operator, Econet Wireless, has formally requested all goods and service suppliers to reduce prices by 20 percent with effect from May 1, 2020, citing the inability to pass rising costs onto consumers due to regulated tariffs that consistently lag inflation and exchange rate movements, with the last tariff adjustment in March 2020 having been rendered sub-economic by inflation surging to 676 percent and the exchange rate falling from 30 to 50 against the US dollar. Econet's letter to suppliers, seen by this publication, explains that suppliers including landlords have pegged charges to the US dollar, causing consistently rising costs while tariffs have remained static since the previous adjustment in October 2019, a gap of approximately five months. The company states: 'As we take this necessary pain to protect our business, we sincerely hope we will share in the benefits when the economy turns around.' Market watchers note that the 20 percent price adjustment will help Econet continue offering uninterrupted services, which is under threat if the company cannot pay suppliers while revenue generated is insufficient to keep the network up and running given increased usage during COVID-19 lockdowns. This proactive cost management initiative follows Government and other economic players agreeing to lower prices to pre-lockdown levels in the same week. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: OK Zim pins hope on improved product supply
content: OK Zimbabwe CEO Alex Siyavora highlighted that sustainable recovery post-COVID-19 lockdown would depend on improved product supply, as shelves were left with few items after consumers stocked up pre-lockdown. The lockdown (declared March 30, 2020) disrupted supply chains, with CZI survey showing 81% of manufacturers experienced supply chain disruptions, production/trading volumes declined 36% on average, and COVID-19 mitigation costs averaged 14% of annual expenditure. OK closed 6 CBD branches due to reduced activity, trading hours reduced to 4-6 hours in provinces, and April daily sales dropped significantly from March. The business expected to struggle covering fixed costs in April, though March was strong from pre-lockdown stockpiling. Siyavora emphasized staying engaged with suppliers and focusing on essential products. Simbisa Brands temporarily suspended contract worker contracts due to low demand. CZI called for policy reforms to support industry post-COVID.

headline: Firms postpone dividends payment
content: African Distillers Limited,"Firms postpone dividends payment; Afdefers interim dividend to May 15, 2020 due to Covid-19 lockdown","Several listed companies have postponed or deferred dividend payments due to the COVID-19 induced lockdown implemented on March 30, 2020, with spirits and wines maker African Distillers Limited being the latest to announce the deferment of its interim dividend payment to May 15, 2020, advising shareholders in a notice that due to the impact of COVID-19 lockdown on the company and the economy at large, the payment date has been deferred while the record date and cum-dividend date remain as previously announced, with the new payment date remaining within the timelines set out in the ZSE listing rules. Simbisa Brands also deferred its interim dividend payment to May 21, 2020, while Meikles deferred payment of its interim dividend to April 30, 2020 with company secretary Thabani Mpofu acknowledging that shareholders will be disappointed in the delay and that consideration will be given to a second interim dividend declaration once the lockdown is over to account for the falling value of money through inflation. Resources group Caledonia Mining Corporation deferred approval of its second quarterly dividend due to elevated global uncertainty, with chairman Leigh Wilson stating the decision was taken out of an abundance of caution until greater clarity emerges on the highly fluid situation. The dividend deferrals are a global phenomenon with the Bank of England having written to UK banks and insurers requesting suspension of dividend payments, resulting in Lloyds, Royal Bank of Scotland, Barclays, HSBC and Standard Chartered holding onto a total of £15.6 billion in expected shareholder payments.

headline: ZSE capitalisation hits $83bn
content: The Zimbabwe Stock Exchange reached an all-time market capitalisation high of $83.8 billion on May 14, 2020 (US$3.35 billion at official rate, US$1.67 billion at parallel rate), with the ZSE All Share Index trading above 600 for the first time since new indices were implemented, recording year-to-date gains of 179.6 percent. The ZSE Top 10 Index (up 168.41% YTD) constitutes 71.34 percent of total market capitalisation. Cassava Smartech is the largest counter at $12.9 billion (15.46% of total), followed by Econet ($12.1 billion), Delta Corporation and OK Zimbabwe. Cafca leads price performance with 1,023 percent gain to 2,000 cents, Art +935.2%, with First Mutual Properties, Medtech and OK Zimbabwe completing the top five performers. Market watchers attribute the rally to hyperinflation (676% in March 2020), cash shortages, a runaway exchange rate, and investors seeking value preservation as the property market struggles with voids and rental arrears, Government bonds offer negative returns, and the money market is unviable due to negative interest rates. CBZ Holdings is not mentioned in this article.

headline: JUST IN: VP Chiwenga tours Tongaat seed maize project
content: Vice President Constantino Chiwenga today toured a seed maize project jointly developed by Lowveld sugar producer Tongaat Huletts Zimbabwe and Seed-Co at Mwenezana Sugar Estates in Mwenezi, covering 465 hectares with capacity to produce over 1,555 tonnes of seed—enough to plant 62,000 hectares of commercial maize. The private sector initiative has potential to produce 600,000 tonnes of maize meeting food needs of roughly 20 percent of Zimbabwe's population. Under the project, Tongaat availed land, irrigation infrastructure and day-to-day crop management while Seed-Co provided specialist agronomic and technical support. VP Chiwenga was accompanied by Agriculture Minister Perrance Shiri, Energy Minister Fortune Chasi, Masvingo Minister of State and senior THZ and Seedco officials. This follows Tongaat's commitment to continue seed maize production and expand winter maize hectarage , with the project part of broader Masvingo winter farming initiatives targeting over 3,000 hectares under maize and traditional grains . VP Chiwenga later emphasized that Zimbabwe should not expect food assistance from other nations given the COVID-19 pandemic, challenging Zimbabweans to fully utilise water bodies and fertile land to achieve food security . The delegation also toured Chilonga Irrigation Scheme, a multi-million dollar lucerne project in southern Chiredzi, and Tugwi-Mukosi Dam in Chivi.

headline: OK Zimbabwe’s annual profit jumps 1 000pc
content: OK Zimbabwe reported profit surge of 1,000 percent to $566 million for the year ended March 31, 2020 from $49 million in the prior year, with profit before tax rising over 1,000 percent to $788.6 million in historical terms, net asset value per share increasing 478 percent to 81.32 cents, and revenue growing 464 percent to $4.5 billion from $801 million in 2019. Chairman Herbert Nkala stated the year was characterised by foreign currency shortages worsening as the year progressed, leading to slowdown in importation of essential capital goods and merchandise, a runaway exchange rate triggering rapid price increases, and inflation soaring to 676 percent (from 66 percent prior year) eroding consumers' real disposable incomes and demand. The COVID-19 pandemic declared in March 2020 resulted in heightened pre-lockdown stocking activity depleting basic products. Overheads grew 427 percent (37 percentage points below revenue growth) driven by generator fuel costs for alternative power due to erratic utilities supplies, electricity costs, maintenance costs and spares, bank charges and rentals. Capital expenditure increased to $236.4 million from $25.8 million for continued refurbishment programme at OK branches in Gweru, Mutare and Triangle, Bon Marche in Westgate, and a new branch opened in Karoi towards year-end. The board declared a final dividend of 9 cents per share, bringing total dividend for the year to 13 cents per share. Prospects are hinged on the duration and severity of the COVID-19 pandemic. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: CBZ dismisses gold buying licence reports
content: CBZ Bank has dismissed as false online publications purporting that the financial institution has been awarded a gold-buying licence by Government and is exporting gold, stating categorically that 'the bank does not hold a gold buying licence neither does it export gold' . The clarification follows media reports claiming CBZ was now buying gold with a target of 1 million tonnes, which the bank refuted while confirming it counts Freda Rebecca Gold Mine among its clients and is offering banking services 'in line with its normal course of business and regulatory requirements' . The distinction is critical: Freda Rebecca Gold Mine—a subsidiary of Landela Investments operating claims spanning 60,000 hectares in Manicaland and Mashonaland provinces—was licenced by Fidelity Printers and Refiners (the country's sole exclusive gold buyer licensed under the Gold Trade Act) to buy gold directly from 500 small-scale and artisanal miners operating within its claims . On the back of this agreement, Freda Rebecca entered into a partnership with CBZ Bank whereby specific CBZ banking halls are converted into specialised gold buying and handling centres, utilising the bank's extensive branch network to locate buying centres as close to miners as possible, with CBZ committing to create tailored products enabling miners to access loans for capital expenditure and working capital, plus investment advisory services to help them grow into mainstream mining firms . Freda Rebecca set an initial target of 500kg monthly with a three-year vision of one tonne monthly . Fidelity Printers and Refiners (now Fidelity Gold Refinery), a wholly-owned subsidiary of the Mutapa Investment Fund, remains Zimbabwe's sole licensed gold buyer and exporter under the Gold Trade Act, with all gold producers required to sell through Fidelity, though the central bank periodically introduces gold export incentives and retention thresholds—including the May 2020 increase to 70 percent foreign currency retention for large-scale producers and US$45 per gramme cash payment for artisanal miners . This distinction—CBZ as banking services provider versus Freda Rebecca as licensed Fidelity agent—resolves the apparent contradiction and confirms CBZ's expanding footprint in mining sector financial services, later evidenced by the bank's US$254 million investment in mining, the 2023 US$200 million Kuvimba Mining House bond mandate (CBZ as financial advisor), and the 2024 US$50 million Afreximbank credit line for mining supply chains

headline: $9m for Covid-19 fight
content: Delta Corporation has spent more than $9 million on corporate social responsibility initiatives aimed at fighting the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic, stating that 'the strength of our business comes from our communities because the majority of our ingredients and products are sourced and brewed locally, we are deeply connected to our communities. Together with the government and partners we're using our existing operations and infrastructure to address immediate needs by developing, donating and distributing essential resources needed to fight the pandemic.' The beverages manufacturer detailed its contributions as $5 million donated to the Covid-19 Response Fund, $2 million worth of non-alcoholic beverages provided to front-line emergency workers, $1.5 million spent on provision of Personal Protective Equipment, and a further $760,000 contributed to Montague Clinic for enhancing health infrastructure. This substantial CSR commitment follows Delta's earlier April 2020 donation of $5 million plus $1 million goods to the COVID-19 Disaster Fund (DLTA030) and extends the company's extensive humanitarian portfolio including RTGS$3.1 million in Cyclone Idai relief (DLTA022, DLTA026).

headline: CBZ capitalises subsidiaries
content: CBZ Holdings confirmed in its Q1 2020 trading update ended March 31, 2020 that all group subsidiaries are now in full compliance with regulatory minimum capital requirements after availing appropriate funding during April 2020 to address shortfalls at CBZ Insurance ($12.4 million) and Redsphere Finance ($1.99 million), bringing them into line with the Insurance and Pensions Commission guidelines and the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe's minimum capital framework requiring commercial banks to hold US$30 million by December 31, 2020, development banks and finance houses US$20 million, deposit-taking microfinance banks US$5 million, and credit-only microfinance banks US$25,000. The group reported significant COVID-19 pandemic impacts including branch and head office closures, employees working from home, declining transactional volumes due to movement restrictions and essential services-only requirements, and impaired debt servicing ability for borrowers in hardest-hit industries such as tourism and hospitality, but assessed that COVID-19 will not have an inhibiting impact on its financial activities and position as most services and business activities have been or can be migrated to online platforms, with the group envisaged to continue as a going concern through aggressive use of digital channels, remote working and limited services. This capitalisation action demonstrates CBZ's proactive regulatory risk management and follows the group's consistent track record of maintaining capital adequacy ratios well above regulatory minimums, including the 58.6 percent capital adequacy ratio reported in Q3 2019 (CBZ005) and the group's historical compliance culture evidenced by former CBZ CEO Dr John Mangudya's subsequent tenure as RBZ Governor (CBZ022)

headline: High Court stays Econet warrant
content: The High Court granted Econet Wireless a provisional order staying execution of a police search warrant that sought the company's entire subscriber database and transaction details (over 11 million customers) as part of a money laundering investigation . Justice Edith Mushore ruled the warrant was impermissibly general, noting it specified 'specific persons alleged to be involved in money laundering' but demanded all customer data, showing police 'may actually not have had reasonable suspicion' . The stay remains until a full hearing determines the warrant's legality. Econet argued the warrant was unconstitutional, violated privacy rights, and was part of a plot to undermine its market leadership . A subsequent final ruling by Justice Clement Phiri (September 2, 2020) declared the warrant unlawful and ordered police to pay costs . The case highlighted tensions between government (which had suspended some EcoCash bulk transactions over currency concerns) and Econet, with CEO Douglas Mboweni accusing presidential adviser Christopher Mutsvangwa of threatening a 'coup' against the company . Rights groups MISA and ZimRights also obtained an order protecting their data

headline: CAFCA exports defy lockdown
content: CAFCA Limited boosted exports over the nine months to June 30, 2020 to 207 tonnes, a 45 percent increase from 143 tonnes in the prior comparable period, notwithstanding a decline in production volumes during the last three months due to the Covid-19 pandemic. The company's primary market is Southern and Central Africa, with an export footprint extending to the European Union including Russia. Overall conductor volumes sold during the third quarter to June 2020 declined to 393 tonnes from 540 tonnes in the prior comparable period, and on a year-to-date basis declined to 1,228 tonnes from 1,384 tonnes over the same period in 2019. Management expressed optimism that volumes will pick up in the fourth quarter to at least match prior-year levels, stating: 'Despite local volumes being 27 percent below the same quarter last year mainly due to the original Covid-19 lockdown, we are still confident that by year-end our volumes will be in line with the previous year.' To ensure local and export sales requirements are met and as a hedge against hyperinflation, CAFCA is increasing finished goods stock levels, which are now currently at 806 tonnes against a monthly sales model of 140 tonnes. This operational update is corroborated by subsequent Q1 2021 trading updates showing export volumes continued their upward trajectory with a 25 percent increase, attributed to 'consignment stock initiatives and Covid-19 implications on logistics', and local volumes increasing 47 percent year-on-year with utilities, mining and retail being very buoyant

headline: Pfumvudza farmers register for inputs
content: PFUMVUDZA FARMERS REGISTER FOR INPUTS AS GOVERNMENT TRAINS 715,000 FARMERS NATIONWIDE FOR 2020 SUMMER CROPPING SEASON UNDER PRESIDENTIAL INPUTS SCHEME WHICH PRIORITISES FARMERS ADOPTING PFUMVUDZA CONSERVATION AGRICULTURE CONCEPT, WITH SEED HOUSES INCLUDING SYNGENTA, AGRISEEDS AND SEED CO CONFIRMING READINESS, ADEQUATE STOCKS AND DISTRIBUTION TO RETAIL OUTLETS AND FARMING AREAS, WHILE FERTILISER INDUSTRY SPOKESMAN MR TAPIWA MASHINGAIDZE WARNS OF FOREIGN CURRENCY CONSTRAINTS AS RAW MATERIALS REMAIN IN BONDED WAREHOUSES REQUIRING FOREIGN CURRENCY FOR RELEASE, WITH MANUFACTURERS RELYING ON AUCTION SYSTEM AND COMPETING WITH MEDICINE AND FUEL IMPORTERS, THREATENING FERTILISER AVAILABILITY AND POTENTIALLY INCREASING PRICES

headline: U.S. Treasury clears CBZ of $385mln penalty
content: The United States Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control has cleared CBZ Holdings of a US$385 million penalty for processing transactions on behalf of ZB Bank Limited—which was under American sanctions from 2008 to 2016—following a comprehensive review of more than 15,000 transactions conducted between 2009 and 2014, with OFAC issuing a cautionary letter dated August 24, 2020 signed by Chief of Regulated Industries Oversight and Evaluation Rachel Dondarski, determining that while CBZ 'appears to have engaged in non-transparent payment practices' by providing indirect access to the US financial system for ZB Bank without referencing ZB's name in payment instructions, the matter has been resolved with a final enforcement response that does not preclude future action should new information warrant . CBZ chairman Marc Holtzman stated that the resolution 'removes a cloud over CBZ, the country and gives comfort to international partners,' noting that as a result of the OFAC issues, CBZ has not had any correspondent relationships with western lenders and management will now begin to re-engage with them . Following the clearance, CBZ successfully established two United States-based correspondent banking relationships by May 2021, significantly improving operations, and announced plans to list on the Victoria Falls Stock Exchange to ignite foreign investor interest and address concerns over trapped funds . The cautionary letter confirmed that CBZ provided OFAC with numerous responses and documents between December 16, 2014 and December 14, 2017, and while urged to exercise greater caution in future compliance with the Zimbabwe Sanctions Regulations, the bank escaped the US$385 million penalty that at the official exchange rate was nearly double the value of CBZ's assets and market capitalisation at the time . This resolution represents the final disposition of the sanctions case first documented in your corpus (CBZ002, CBZ070, CBZ078) and validates the diplomatic advocacy efforts by President Mnangagwa and Minister Ncube at the June 2019 US-Africa Business Summit (CBZ078) as well as chairman Holtzman's strategic engagement of law firm Dentons and assembly of a new management team following his September 2019 appointment

headline: OK Zim volumes slide on Covid-19 lockdown
content: OK Zimbabwe Limited's sales volume for the quarter ended June 30, 2020 declined 32 percent compared to the similar quarter in the prior year as the market grappled with low disposable incomes due to effects of COVID-19, with the national lockdown implemented March 30, 2020 reducing trading hours, disrupting production and supply to stores, and constraining activity in stores. The pandemic hit an already fragile economy characterised by foreign currency shortages, fuel supply challenges, erratic utilities supplies and high inflation resulting in waning disposable incomes, with annual inflation rising from 676 percent in March to 737 percent in June. Currency depreciation resulted in frequent price increases in a situation of limited disposable incomes leading to subdued demand. Revenue for the period was 13 percent below the same period in the prior year in inflation-adjusted terms but rose 661 percent in historical terms. While management remains upbeat of maintaining profitability, the effects of COVID-19 pandemic are likely to persist causing challenges to the economic environment. For the year to March 31, 2020, overheads grew 427 percent (37 percentage points below revenue growth) driven by generator fuel costs for alternative power, electricity costs, maintenance costs and spares, bank charges and rentals. Profit surged 1,000 percent to $566 million from $49 million, net asset value per share rose 478 percent to 81.32 cents from 14.05 cents, and revenue for the year was 464 percent above the $801 million recorded in 2019 to $4.5 billion. Market watchers expect foreign currency shortages and fuel supply constraints to continue in the near future, affecting business operations with increased overheads and other operating costs. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, Seed Co, Hippo Valley Estates, BAT Zimbabwe, Dairiboard Zimbabwe, CAFCA Limited, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: OK suspends Grand Challenge
content: OK Zimbabwe announced the suspension of its flagship OK Grand Challenge Jackpot Promotion for 2020 due to COVID-19 pandemic disruptions. The promotion, which has run for over three decades, is the country's biggest retail promotion. The suspension follows Q1 FY2021 (ended June 30, 2020) sales volume decline of 32% compared to the prior year, as the market grappled with low disposable incomes due to COVID-19 effects and lockdown restrictions. Despite challenges, the group maintained reasonable stock levels since lockdown began March 30, 2020. The retailer implemented COVID-19 safety measures including PPE for staff, temperature checks, customer limits, social distancing protocols, and supplier engagement to ensure merchandise availability. This marks the second time in its history the promotion has been suspended (previous suspension in 2009 during economic crisis). The company continues operating as an essential service provider but with reduced trading hours and restricted consumer movement impacting performance.

headline: Bourse on weak path
content: ZSE market value plunges $22bn or 9.6% in three days post-reopening","The Zimbabwe Stock Exchange experienced sustained selling pressure in the first three days after reopening from a month-long suspension following allegations the bourse was being used to sabotage the economy, with total market value plunging 9.6 percent or $22 billion to settle at $206 billion, the ZSE All Share Index falling 9 percent to 1,616.38 points, the ZSE Top 10 Index declining 12.05 percent to 1,084 points, the ZSE Top 15 Index weakening 11 percent to 1,254 points, the Medium Cap slowing 5 percent to 3,118 points, and the Small Cap suffering the biggest casualty with a 12.26 percent decline to 4,876 points, while three counters—Old Mutual, Seed Co International and PPC—remained suspended. Dragging the market into negative territory were RioZim (-43% to $6.40), MedTech (-31% to 8.01c), Delta Corporation and Powerspeed (-28% each to $17.73 and $1.03) with Delta recently reporting beer volume declines for the quarter to June 30, 2020 on low disposable incomes and COVID-19 disruptions, Masimba (-25% to $1.25), and Willdale, Padenga and Meikles (-21% each to 22c, $10.83 and $13), while gains were recorded in Zimplow (+9% to $3.33), ZB (+5% to $12) on total income rising 144 percent driven by fair value credits and foreign exchange gains, FMP (+2% to $2.10), ZHL (+1% to $2.50), Dawn (+0.8% to $1.15), Afdis (+0.34% to $18.30), and Bindura (+0.22% to $3.94). CBZ Holdings is not mentioned in this article.

headline: BAT’s tax contribution in massive 769pc jump
content: BAT Zimbabwe chairman Mr Lovemore Manatsa reported that the company's contribution to Zimbabwe Revenue Authority in taxes increased 769 percent to $173 million for the half year ended June 30, 2020 from $19.9 million in the prior comparable period, with key contributors being excise duty and corporate tax driven by increases in selling prices and profit before tax. Despite total sales volumes declining 3 percent from the prior comparable period—largely attributable to shrinking consumer disposable incomes and the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic lockdown affecting movements of people and visits to recreational facilities where products are normally consumed—the premium brand Dunhill returned to the market as the company was now able to import, recording a significant 184 percent increase versus the same period in prior year. Newbury volumes declined 10 percent, value for money segment (Madison and Everest) recorded 1 percent increase, and low value for money brand Ascot recorded 40 percent decrease. Group revenues rose 20 percent to $410.5 million driven by price increases and revenue from export of cut-rag, resulting in gross profit increase of $49.6 million. Selling and marketing costs decreased by $0.2 million driven by route to market initiatives, administrative expenses were $17 million lower (35%) due to ongoing cost saving initiatives, while other losses increased by $116.4 million due to foreign exchange losses on liabilities driven by Zimbabwe dollar devaluation. Operating profit was 498 percent higher, net profit attributable to shareholders was $73.7 million compared to $18.8 million loss in prior period, and earnings per share increased to $3.58 from $0.91. Cash generated from operations was negative $13.1 million due to significant increase in trade and other receivables from prepayments to purchase leaf and increased debtors' book from price increases. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, Seed Co, Hippo Valley Estates, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: ZETDC seeks stiffer cable theft penalties
content: The Zimbabwe Electricity Transmission and Distribution Company is seeking a review of sentences for cable and transformer theft from 10 years to 30 years to curb vandalism costing Zesa Holdings over US$2 million annually, with ZETDC requiring more than US$14 million to procure intruder detection gadgets to fight the scourge, according to Energy and Power Development Deputy Minister Magna Mudyiwa in the National Assembly. Deputy Minister Mudyiwa confirmed that ZETDC is in the process of copper harvesting where the power utility has an arrangement with Central African Cables (Pvt) Ltd (CAFCA) where copper is exchanged for aluminium conductors, a deal that has generated significant controversy and regulatory scrutiny since 2014 when the Competition and Tariff Commission first investigated potential unfair trade practices . The CTC alleged the barter agreement restricted competition in the market for distribution of electricity conductors, and in November 2018 condemned the deal as a restrictive practice, requesting the Procurement Regulatory Authority of Zimbabwe to withdraw authority granted to CAFCA and ZETDC and recommending that ZETDC flight open tenders for aluminium conductor cables . Despite these objections, the deal remained operational, and in November 2020 ZESA executive chairman Sidney Gata faced renewed criticism over an internal memo instructing the company's managing director to enter into a copper harvesting contract with CAFCA, with Gata defending the meeting and stating that the arrangement was approved by PRAZ years earlier and CAFCA was the only organisation capable of recycling copper for conductor production . By 2022, CAFCA reported that volumes had been adversely affected by delays in getting regulatory approval to extend the barter deal with ZETDC, impacting the company's ability to cut copper import costs . Deputy Minister Mudyiwa also noted that ZETDC plans to install intruder detection systems on approximately 27,000 distribution transformers nationwide at an estimated cost of US$13.5 million, with 80 transformers installed as a pilot project and contracts in place to install 8,000 sites in 2020, while Government lobbies for cancellation of copper licences which perpetuate theft as dealers sell stolen copper as scrap."

headline: Banks ready to recover lost market
content: CBZ Holdings 22% market share poised to benefit","Morgan & Co analysts state that the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe's recent restrictions on mobile money platforms—including limiting mobile wallet users to one account per individual, capping daily mobile money transfers at $5,000, banning mobile money agents, restricting merchants from making payments from their wallets, and requiring regulatory approval for bulk payments—have inadvertently given traditional banks an opportunity to regain market share lost to mobile money platforms which accounted for 84.8 percent of all transaction volumes and 22.6 percent of value in Q4 2019, with active mobile financial services subscribers reaching 6.54 million. CBZ Holdings is explicitly identified as the market leader with 22 percent market share alongside Stanbic (15 percent) and CABS (14 percent), with analysts noting that economies of scale will further work to the advantage of banks with huge market share including CBZ Holdings, FBC Holdings, Old Mutual Zimbabwe and ZB Financial Holdings. CBZ Bank has launched digital banking channels facilitated by web portals and mobile apps as part of significant IT infrastructure investment aimed at 'bringing the bank to you', while addressing legacy issues of physical branch networks through closures and digital migration accelerated by COVID-19. The analysts note that while mobile money firms are skidding on a slippery regulatory environment, traditional banks are upping their digital game, setting the stage for a bruising battle for dominance of the local financial services industry. This strategic positioning follows CBZ's earlier fintech and cryptocurrency exploration under chairman Marc Holtzman (CBZ093) and precedes the group's continued digital transformation under Group CEO Lawrence Nyazema's financial inclusion initiatives targeting the informal sector (CBZ080)

headline: Econet, RBH partner on African solar projects
content: Telecoms giant Econet and Royal Bafokeng Holdings have announced a partnership to develop up to 175MW of solar energy assets in selected African countries, with RBH investing up to US$20 million through Distributed Power Africa—Econet's subsidiary focused on developing and operating renewable energy assets across Africa. DPA works with commercial and industrial businesses to adopt hybrid clean energy solutions using lithium battery technology to improve energy security, reduce costs and improve carbon footprint, with the investment providing funding to boost DPA's commercial and industrial solar projects in South Africa, Kenya, Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Zambia. DPA Africa chief executive Norman Moyo stated the company has sought ways to ease the burden of deploying solar by ensuring customers engage in a capex-free model with power lease agreements of up to 15 years, describing the partnership with like-minded African investor RBH as exciting to unlock access to sustainable energy, noting that access to energy is a key driver of Econet's vision of a digitally connected future that leaves no African behind. RBH chief executive Albertina Kekana stated the partnership enhances energy as a key enabler for growth of sustainable data centres—now a major investment focus in RBH's portfolio—as demand for connectivity has been greatly increased by the pandemic, and RBH has partnered with a very dynamic business with a strong record of execution on the African continent backed by a very successful Group, Econet. The partnership comes at an opportune time when the South African Government is encouraging investment in renewable energy and will accelerate the much-needed 6.8GW energy capacity outlined in the Integrated Resource Plan of 2019. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: ZSE records 17pc turnover in September
content: CBZ falls 1.8%, Afdis drops 15% in weekly decline","The Zimbabwe Stock Exchange closed September 2020 with strong monthly performance, recording 17.5 percent market capitalisation growth to $206.5 billion with turnover reaching $4.6 billion—the highest single month turnover in years and more than double the previous high of 379.97 million shares traded in June 2020—yet the final week to September 30 saw subdued demand drag four benchmark indices into losses as the ZSE Top 10 fell 3.57 percent, the ZSE All Share Index declined 2.64 percent, and total market value retreated 2.57 percent to $206 billion, with contrasting performance across key counters. CBZ Holdings, the market's largest counter by value, fell 1.8 percent to $52.90, while African Distillers Limited dropped 15 percent to $17 as part of the week's significant decliners alongside Turnall (-35% to 52c), FCB and Nampak (-33% each), Mashonaland Holdings (-32% to 41.75c), Padenga and Ariston (-17% each), and Dairibord, Meikles and Seed Co (-13% each) with Dairibord reporting half-year sales volumes falling 32 percent, revenue down 14 percent and raw milk intake declining 6 percent due to economic volatility worsened by COVID-19. Weekly gains were recorded in FBC (+28% to $15.43), ZPI (+21% to 70c), Fidelity (+18% to 96c) with both Fidelity and ZPI being consolidated into ZHL subject to shareholder approval, African Sun (+17% to $1.89), Edgars (+15% to $1), ZB (+13% to $15.60), Zimplow (+10% to $4.20), Masimba (+8% to $1.90), GetBucks and Lafarge (+4% each), and Bindura (+3% to $4.10), while NTS, MedTech, General Beltings, ZHL and Zimpapers remained flat with Zimpapers reporting digital platforms playing a critical role in reaching readers as COVID-19 lockdown negatively affected traditional retail and street sales channels.

headline: ZSE in record monthly turnover
content: CBZ falls 1.8%, Afdis drops 15% in weekly decline","The Zimbabwe Stock Exchange recorded its highest single month turnover in years during September 2020, with turnover reaching $4.6 billion—more than double the $1.9 billion traded in June 2020—and market capitalisation gaining $30.8 billion or 7.5 percent to close at $206.5 billion, while the number of shares traded at 1.09 billion more than doubled the previous June high of 379.97 million, yet the final week to September 30 saw subdued demand drag four benchmark indices into losses as the ZSE Top 10 fell 3.57 percent, the ZSE All Share Index declined 2.64 percent, and total market value retreated 2.57 percent to $206 billion, with contrasting performance across key counters. CBZ Holdings, the market's largest counter by value, fell 1.8 percent to $52.90, while African Distillers Limited dropped 15 percent to $17 as part of the week's significant decliners alongside Turnall (-35% to 52c), FCB and Nampak (-33% each), Mashonaland Holdings (-32% to 41.75c), Padenga and Ariston (-17% each), and Dairibord, Meikles and Seed Co (-13% each) with Dairibord reporting half-year sales volumes falling 32 percent, revenue down 14 percent and raw milk intake declining 6 percent due to economic volatility worsened by COVID-19. Weekly gains were recorded in FBC (+28% to $15.43), ZPI (+21% to 70c), Fidelity (+18% to 96c) with both Fidelity and ZPI being consolidated into ZHL subject to shareholder approval, African Sun (+17% to $1.89), Edgars (+15% to $1), ZB (+13% to $15.60), Zimplow (+10% to $4.20), Masimba (+8% to $1.90), GetBucks and Lafarge (+4% each), and Bindura (+3% to $4.10), while NTS, MedTech and General Beltings remained flat.

headline: European stocks fall
content: CBZ jumps 13% to $43.91 as ZSE gains continue","Positive momentum remained on the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange during the week to October 21, 2020 with four of the benchmark indices closing in positive territory as the ZSE All Share Index gained 0.79 percent to 1,516 points, the ZSE Top 10 Index rose 1.53 percent to 988 points, the ZSE Top 15 Index gained 0.78 percent to 1,166 points, and the Small Cap paced fastest with a 3.17 percent increase to 6,143 points, while the Medium Cap was the only index to close negative giving up 0.62 percent to 3,026 points, as total market value added $1 billion or 0.6 percent to $191.8 billion reflective of gains recorded across board. The market's biggest counter by market capitalisation, CBZ Holdings, jumped 13 percent to $43.91, joining significant gainers including Willdale and Fidelity (+20% each to 24c and $1.38), Truworths (+19% to 15.7c), ZB (+18% to $17.78), African Sun (+16.45% to $1.80), Powerspeed (+16.1% to $1.80), FMP (+6% to $2.35), and FCB, Masimba and Star Africa (+5% each to 54.88c, $2.58 and 18c), while declines were recorded in Proplastics (-20% to $4.76), Nampak (-19% to 84.25c), RTG (-11% to $2), Bindura (-8% to $3.65), RioZim (-6.6% to $8.30), Axia, Mashonaland Holdings and Turnall (-6% each), Econet, Delta and Zimpapers (-5% each to $4.35, $14.98 and 67.88c), and Cassava and Dawn (-2% each) with capital markets regulator SECZIM investigating the proposed Dawn-African Sun transaction over concerns of potential prejudice to minority shareholders. Afdis remained unchanged at $16.37, while Edgars, GetBucks, Lafarge, Natfoods, TSL and ZPI also maintained prior week levels.

headline: Econet marginally adjusts Bundle of Joy tariffs
content: Econet Wireless Zimbabwe marginally adjusted its 'Bundle of Joy' voice bundle tariffs on November 25, 2020, increasing the popular daily bundle from ZW$6.00 (approx. US$0.07) for two minutes by only 20% [citation:4]. This increase was significantly below the annual inflation rate of 475.21% for October 2020 [citation:4][citation:10]. The company, which has invested over US$1.3 billion in infrastructure since 2009, stated the adjustment was necessary to guarantee continued delivery of acceptable service to customers [citation:1][citation:5][citation:10]. The tariff review occurred amid government calls for ICT sector development to achieve Vision 2030 targets, with ICT Minister Jenfan Muswere emphasizing the need for universal access and 5G readiness [citation:1][citation:4]. Analysts noted that cost-reflective tariffs and tax reductions are essential for telecoms to reinvest in capital-intensive infrastructure [citation:4][citation:10]. The sector faces hyperinflation, rising operating costs (including a 50% electricity price hike), and competition from over-the-top services [citation:4][citation:5][citation:10].

headline: BAT feels Covid-19 heat
content: BAT Zimbabwe recorded an 8 percent decline in overall sales volumes for the nine months ended September 30, 2020 compared to the same period last year due to waning consumer spend and effects of the COVID-19 pandemic, with the national lockdown from March 30, 2020 reducing business trading hours and imposing movement restrictions on an already challenging business environment characterised by foreign currency shortages and high inflation, according to chairman Lovemore Manatsa. Premium brand Dunhill returned to the market recording a 962 percent increase compared to the same period in prior year. Aspirational premium brands Newbury and Kingsgate volumes declined 33 percent, value for money segment (Madison and Everest) declined 5 percent, and low value for money brand Ascot declined 43 percent. Turnover increased 31 percent in inflation accounting terms driven by price increases taken during the period and revenue generated from export of cut-rag tobacco. Manatsa stated: 'While the trading conditions are expected to remain challenging for the rest of 2020 mainly driven by macro-economic variables and the effects of the Covid-19 pandemic, the company believes that it will deliver value growth for its shareholders,' pinning hopes on the foreign currency auction system introduced June 2020 to enhance access to foreign currency in a fair and transparent manner and bring stability to the exchange rate. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned

headline: CBZ deal boosts ZSE’s turnover
content: A foreign investor acquired a 1.2 percent stake in CBZ Holdings on November 10, 2020, purchasing 8,712,767 shares valued at $374.6 million (US$4.6 million) at an unchanged price of $43.90 per share, in a transaction that significantly boosted the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange's overall turnover to $436.5 million. The deal contributed to positive market sentiment with the All Share Index closing 0.81 percent higher at 1,520.83 points, while Delta Corporation recorded a marginal 0.17 percent gain to $14.77 and Innscor rose 13.31 percent to $24.49, offset by SeedCo which fell 11.34 percent as the session's steepest decliner. This foreign investor acquisition represents continued international interest in CBZ following chairman Marc Holtzman's diplomatic advocacy campaign (CBZ016) and the bank's US$14 million to US$500 million market capitalisation re-rating (CBZ010), and precedes the group's December 2023 strategic leadership transition where Holtzman retired and Dr Blessing Mudavanhu stepped down as Group CEO (CBZ079).

headline: Project strengthens agricultural education, extension, and research
content: The Zimbabwe Agricultural Knowledge and Innovation Services (ZAKIS) project, part of the European Union-funded Zimbabwe Agricultural Growth Programme (2018-2024), has made significant contributions to strengthening agricultural education, extension and research services in Zimbabwe, led by the Ministry of Lands, Agriculture, Water and Rural Resettlement and supported by a consortium including Welthungerhilfe, ICRISAT, Community Technology Development Organisation and Sustainable Agriculture Technology. Agricultural Centres of Excellence established at Chibero Agricultural College and Matopos Research Institute, with District Agricultural Centres of Excellence in Insiza, Mhondoro-Ngezi, Matobo and Chegutu, link over 100,000 farmers to private sector partners including Seed Co (through Prime SeedCo and SeedCo) , National Foods, Superfert Fertilizer Company, Klein Karoo (K2), Fish Feeds, and Dupont Pioneer/Pannar through learning events, innovation platforms, demonstrations, field days and farmer training workshops. The project has refurbished animal husbandry facilities, established crop demonstration plots, and equipped the Matopos ACE with a state-of-the-art nutrition laboratory, solar system for administration block, and solar-powered boreholes for drip irrigation at demonstration plots. An e-Extension strategy is being developed including the upcoming launch of ZimAgriHub (farmer-focused national online library), two fully equipped podcasting studios at ACEs, and an In-Service Training Application, with extension officers to receive smartphones and tablets for digital resource access. ICRISAT-led action research has resulted in new sorghum and pearl millet varieties that are more resilient to drought and better adapted to semi-arid areas now available on the local market. The project is also supporting development of a new Agriculture Education, Extension and Research Policy through participatory research and stakeholder consultation meetings across Mashonaland West, Mashonaland Central, Manicaland, Masvingo and Midlands, and a review of agriculture college curricula in partnership with FAO-SAFE.

headline: Covid-19 fails to slowdown OK Zim
content: OK Zimbabwe continued store refurbishments and new openings despite COVID-19 pandemic uncertainties, with capital expenditure surging to $384.9 million for the half year ended September 30, 2020 (up from $51.5 million) and capital commitments of $2 billion. Post-reporting period, the group opened a new OKmart store in Victoria Falls on October 1, 2020 and an OK Store in Harare's Sanganayi Inn area on November 5, 2020, with eight stores targeted for completion by year-end. This counter-cyclical investment came despite sales volume decline of 26.9 percent for the six months due to COVID-19 containment measures, reduced operating hours, runway local currency depreciation, and inflation peaking at 837.5 percent in July. Revenue grew 684 percent to $8.7 billion, profit before tax up 621 percent to $1.3 billion, profit after tax increased 634 percent to $968.3 million, and overheads grew 680.6 percent driven by COVID-19 response costs including RDT and PCR tests, face masks, thermometers, hand sanitisers, staff passage costs, contingent rentals, repairs and maintenance, bank charges, and cleaning expenses. Cash and cash equivalents declined to $200 million from $601 million in inflation-adjusted terms, yet the group declared an interim dividend of 26 cents per share. This follows OK Zimbabwe's resilient performance through the pandemic period, including the OK Grand Challenge Jackpot Promotion launch (OKZ005) and continued strategic investment in physical footprint despite challenging operating conditions

headline: SeedCo International projects US$4,8m profit
content: Seed Co International Limited projects profit before tax of US$4.8 million for the half year ended September 30, 2020, representing a 275-295 percent improvement from the US$1.6 million loss before tax recorded in the prior comparable period, driven primarily by early maize seed sales, with interim results expected to be published on or before December 31, 2020. The company, which became the first counter to list on the Victoria Falls Stock Exchange in October 2020 following its delisting from the ZSE , is simultaneously proposing to acquire the entire issued share capital of its Zimbabwean subsidiary Seed Co Limited in a deal estimated at $5.1 billion (US$62.2 million). The transaction proposes an open market offer to acquire up to 247,169,845 SCL shares, settled through the issuance of new SCIL shares on the basis of 1 SCIL ordinary share for every 0.98 SCL ordinary shares held . The board, chaired by David Edward Beaumont Long, cited the need to protect shareholder value as SCIL trades in USD on VFEX while SCL trades in ZWL on ZSE, and the requirement to have underlying Zimbabwean operations to justify raising capital on VFEX under SI 196 of 2020 incentives . SCIL shareholders approved the proposed transaction on December 31, 2020 . Subsequent financial data confirms the company achieved net income of US$10.88 million for FY2021 and US$6.98 million for FY2022 , while a November 2025 trading update projected interim EPS improvement of 70-90% on stronger cash generation and credit risk management . No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned

headline: Delta Beverages loses case against SA firm
content: Delta Beverages failed to make a case against South African company Blakey Investments (Pvt) Ltd after the High Court upheld the agreement between the two parties signed on March 16, 2018 for the supply of plastic packaging, with Delta required to purchase a minimum quantity. When Blakey Investments sought payment of R150,000 for goods supplied pursuant to the agreement, Delta refused and approached the High Court seeking an order declaring the agreement void and unenforceable for allegedly breaching competition laws and Exchange Control Regulations (Statutory Instrument 109 of 1966). Blakey Investments argued that the agreement contained a severance clause allowing any offending part to be removed and that the same clause enjoined parties to negotiate amendments, with Blakey having sought to engage Delta in June 2020 to propose amendments to offending clauses, to which Delta never responded. High Court Justice Webster Chinamora dismissed Delta's application with costs on an attorney and client scale, ruling that Delta failed to establish a basis for the declaratory order, that nothing prevented the parties from removing exclusivity provisions given their history of ad hoc business relationships, that the agreement's built-in negotiation procedure had not been utilised as Delta failed to respond to Blakey's engagement attempt, and that nothing in the agreement implicated a breach of Exchange Control Regulations to undermine its validity. Justice Chinamora stated: 'In all the circumstances, I am satisfied that the disputed agreement was properly signed between the parties, and that no basis has been established for the declaratory order which seeks to nullify it.

headline: Econet data traffic jumps 90pc on Covid-19
content: Econet Wireless Zimbabwe reported an 89.5% surge in data traffic volumes for the quarter ended November 30, 2020 compared to the same period in 2019, driven by COVID-19 lockdown restrictions accelerating digitalization across schools and businesses. SMS volumes also grew on increased e-commerce adoption driving customer interactions. Voice traffic volumes declined following tariff adjustments in July/August 2020 but began recovering. The company noted that past investment in resilient network infrastructure positioned it as the 'digital connectivity partner of choice,' enabling customers to work and learn from home. Econet stated that inflation-adjusted price approvals from POTRAZ helped restore sector viability, though accumulated foreign obligations in the telecommunications sector continue to prevent capitalization of operators. Zimbabwe implemented tightened lockdown restrictions from January 2021 including a 6am-6pm curfew and banning of non-essential businesses as COVID-19 cases surged.

headline: BAT named among global top employers
content: BAT Zimbabwe was named in the top 10 of the latest nominations by the Top Employers Institute, a global entity recognizing excellence in workplace conditions for over 30 years, certifying organizations across 120 countries. BAT, manufacturer of Madison, Everest and Dunhill brands, received recognition at a time when Government is calling on private sector to create rewarding working environments aligned with Vision 2030. Top Employers Institute CEO David Plink commended BAT South Africa and Southern African Markets for prioritizing employee welfare despite the challenging year. BAT Zimbabwe General Manager Kimesh Naidoo stated the certification demonstrates the company continues to deliver workplace conditions of the highest standard, providing a diverse workforce with an inclusive environment where they can thrive. The recognition applies to BAT's Southern African Markets region, including Zimbabwe.

headline: SeedCo hosts sugar bean production symposium
content: Seed Co hosted a virtual sugar bean production symposium to enhance productivity, sustainability, and boost Zimbabwe's food security. The company is partnering with NGOs and donors to create profitable businesses that benefit and sustain farmers through knowledge sharing and product showcasing. The symposium comes as Seed Co directs capital expenditure toward an artificial seed dryer project (US$10 million facility), though completion has been delayed by foreign currency shortages, liquidity challenges, and COVID-19 restrictions. The dryer aims to reduce post-harvest losses and enable farmers to utilize land at least twice yearly. Seed Co has developed a number of sugar bean varieties. The company operates in Nigeria, Zambia, Rwanda, Malawi, Kenya, and Tanzania. [Later in 2021, Seed Co would launch Gandra, a new sugar bean variety described as early maturing (2 months) with yield capacity of 3 tonnes per hectare.]

headline: Govt guarantees $1bn business sector loans
content: Finance and Economic Development Minister Professor Mthuli Ncube issued Government guarantees in an Extraordinary Gazette dated February 26, 2021 for bonds and loans worth approximately $1 billion secured by IDBZ ($300m bonds), Agribank ($200m agrobills), Agribank and FBC jointly ($100m agrobills), Silo Foods Industries ($350m bills), and CBZ Agro-Yield (Private) Limited, binding Government as surety for up to 50 percent of the indebtedness owed by 2020 winter maize farmers in the event of default, with the guarantee valid until December 31, 2020. This gazetting formalised the guarantee framework for the 2019/20 and 2020/21 agricultural seasons documented throughout your corpus (CBZ015, CBZ030, CBZ033, CBZ041, CBZ043). Subsequent public debt bulletins and investigative reports (2022-2024) revealed the extensive default experience underlying these guarantees: the $76.8 million 2020 winter maize facility (50% partial guarantee) recorded a 0.6% recovery rate as at September 2022, with the guarantee called up and fully paid, though post-guarantee recoveries later reached $100 million ; the $4.5 billion 2019/20 maize facility (100% guarantee) achieved only 38.5% recovery, requiring Government to assume $2.77 billion in unpaid balances ; the US$275 million 2020/21 summer maize facility (80% partial guarantee) had a 20.5% recovery rate as at September 2022, with a US$188 million guarantee claim called up and settlement in progress ; and the US$66.6 million 2021 winter wheat facility (73% guarantee) recorded 40% recovery, with a US$29 million guarantee claim called up . By September 2024, Treasury had settled approximately 84% of the US$356 million advanced under these programmes, with outstanding balances of US$59 million, utilising offsetting deposits with CBZ (US$107.8 million) and promissory notes (US$80.17 million maturing quarterly to June 30, 2025) . CBZ Agro-Yield subsequently underwent a 'very painful' restructuring, tightening lending criteria towards 'stable farmers' and strengthening internal systems . Minister Masuka later warned that defaulting farmers would be excluded from future programmes, with those having outstanding debts required to pay at least 50% of the 2020/21 summer season loan amounts by September 30, 2021 to remain eligible

headline: No maize at commodities exchange
content: CBZ Holdings named key partner in Zimbabwe Mercantile Exchange","The Zimbabwe Mercantile Exchange (ZMX) will comply with Statutory Instrument 145 of 2019 which designates the Grain Marketing Board as the sole trader of maize, prohibiting private trading of strategic grains on the commodities exchange while allowing trading of non-strategic grains and commodities including ground nuts, according to Financial and Securities Exchange Limited general manager Mr Garikayi Munema during a stakeholders monthly progress update meeting. Critically, the article confirms that ZMX is a partnership between Government and the private sector led by FINSEC, TSL Limited and CBZ Holdings, with further technical assistance from the Food and Agricultural Organisation and Indaba Agricultural Policy Research Institute on integrating smallholder farmers into the mainstream economy. The exchange has finalised on-boarding of participants including custodian and settlement banks, farmers, warehouse receipt financiers, commodity off-takers, end users, regulators, quality inspectors and warehouses, with GMB providing warehouses alongside TSL Limited, Origen and ETG, while the Warehouse Receipt System allows farmers to store commodities and pay storage and transportation costs later upon sale, tracking their position through a dedicated system. This confirms CBZ's strategic positioning across Zimbabwe's evolving agricultural financing and marketing architecture, complementing its Command Agriculture mandate (CBZ030, CBZ033, CBZ041), CBZ Agro-Yield operational platform (CBZ017, CBZ024, CBZ035, CBZ069), AMA agro-bill issuance (CBZ068), and the independently verified 160,000 hectares for maize and soya production (CBZ025). The ZMX launch scheduled for March 31, 2021 coincides with Zimbabwe's expected bumper harvest, with GMB completing silo refurbishments across the country to handle anticipated large grain volumes.

headline: BAT full-year sales volumes drop 12pc
content: BAT Zimbabwe reported a 12 percent drop in sales volume for the full year ended December 31, 2020, with management stating inflationary pressures eroded consumer purchasing power and the COVID-19 pandemic impacted sales. Low value for money brand Ascot declined 47 percent, aspirational premium segment (Dunhill, Kingsgate, Dunhill Newbury) declined 45 percent, and value for money segment (Madison and Everest) eased 8 percent. Premium brand Dunhill recorded a 1,481 percent volume increase following its return to the market in March 2020 after the company struggled to import it in the previous financial year. Revenue increased 40 percent to $2 billion in inflation-adjusted terms driven by price increases and revenue generated from export of cut-rag tobacco, resulting in gross profit of $1.071 billion (up 2 percent from $1.053 billion in 2019). Revenue growth was lower than cost growth reflecting inflationary pressures. Selling and marketing costs increased 118 percent to $149 million driven by additional marketing investments and strategic initiatives responding to consumer preferences. Administrative expenses increased 156 percent to $335 million driven by general cost increases. Other losses increased $106 million from $276 million due to local currency devaluation and foreign exchange losses on foreign creditors. The company recorded a monetary gain of $183 million compared to a $524 million loss prior year, resulting in profit after tax of $61 million against prior year loss of $124 million, with basic earnings per share of $3.51 against prior year loss per share of $7.15. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: Ok Grand Challenge race back
content: Ok Grand Challenge race back; horse race returns","The 36th edition of the OK Grand Challenge horse race will return after a one-year absence due to COVID-19, with the race set to be held virtually as part of ways to combat the spread of the pandemic. Zimbabwe's biggest retail group OK Zimbabwe launched this year's edition of the OK Grand Challenge Jackpot Promotion at Royal Harare yesterday despite a challenging operating environment. The OK Grand Challenge was last held in 2019 with 15 runners taking to the starting stalls, with the last race won by Simona of The Bridget Stidolph yard. This article is primarily a sports announcement regarding the horse race event and contains no material financial or operational information about OK Zimbabwe Limited as a listed entity. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned."

headline: Record runners in OK Grand Challenge final field
content: "A record 20 horses have been accepted for the 2021 OK Grand Challenge horse race at Borrowdale Park on June 5, with a sponsorship purse of US$70,000 marking the return of the 33-year-old prestigious event after its 2020 cancellation due to COVID-19. The winner will receive US$35,000, first runner-up US$17,500, third-place US$10,000 and fourth-place US$7,000. For the first time in race history, 31 horses were nominated and 28 declared runners, requiring eliminations to reach the final field of 18 led by Gokhan Terzi's Tandava (the only Zimbabwean horse), Bridget Stidolph's Wantage, Kirk Swanson's Diesel and Dust, Armstrong, Holy Land and local favourite Dindingwe, with Fortuna Doro and Christofle as reserves. OK Zimbabwe Commercial Director Albert Katsande welcomed the return of the race after the event was cancelled last year because of Covid-19.
