This follows the OK Grand Challenge Jackpot Promotion launch in April 2021 (OKZ005) and continues OK Zimbabwe's longstanding association with the country's biggest promotion and its flagship horse racing sponsorship, despite the challenging operating environment."

headline: CBZ goes big on agriculture
content: CBZ Holdings chief executive officer Dr Blessing Mudavanhu has detailed the bank's transformational agricultural lending system that almost completely eliminates defaults by providing farmers with inputs equivalent to the loan value rather than cash, partnering with suppliers such as Seed Co to deliver exact quantities suited to each farmer's hectarage, and controlling the entire value chain—suppliers, farmers and off-takers—within sight as all are clients of the bank, creating a closed loop that mitigates credit risk. The bank recorded an 876 percent increase in total advances to $29.4 billion for the year ended December 30, 2020, with a significant chunk channelled to agriculture, and the CBZ Agro-Yield division launched in 2019 recorded a net profit of $299 million, with agriculture a key contributor to the group's 1,893 percent growth in total income. Chairman Mr Marc Holtzman stated this strategy is transformational, having eliminated corruption and repayment risk, significantly reduced non-performing loans, and made financing more efficient by directing resources to worthy and deserving farmers who are producing and adding to the economy, noting that agriculture remains vital for food security and agro-industry, contributes approximately 17 percent to GDP, provides employment for 60-70 percent of the population, supplies 60 percent of industrial raw materials, and contributes 40 percent of total export earnings. CBZ Agro-Yield completed contracting 49,377 hectares for the 2021 winter cropping season, accounting for 78 percent of the national target of 60,000 hectares, confirming the bank's dominant role in wheat production financing which later delivered a record harvest of over 337,000 tonnes—the highest in 20 years. The lender is also involved in mechanisation and links farmers to markets with agronomic support, farm visits and crop monitoring to further reduce default risk.

headline: Why dryland rice farming must take root in Zim
content: Seed Co releases two rice varieties","Seed Co's global head for Research and Development, Dr Gorden Mabuyaye, announced the release of two new rice varieties (SCOP02 and SCOP04) suitable for Zimbabwe's local dryland farming conditions, following successful trials and assessment. The varieties have been licensed and seed is currently being bulked in preparation for farmers intending to undertake rice production in upcoming agricultural seasons. Zimbabwe currently spends US$80-100 million annually on rice imports (over 250,000 tonnes) while local production stands at a mere one tonne. Dr Mabuyaye stated: 'With the idea of saving the country millions of dollars in import fees in mind, we undertook research and development of these two rice varieties which upon completion tried and tested in local climatic conditions and rainfall patterns. The pilot project on the seed was successfully and we licensed it. Currently we are in the process of bulking up seed in preparation for those farmers who intend to undertake its production in the next agricultural seasons.' This strategic development follows Seed Co's extensive research portfolio including new maize hybrid varieties released in Zambia (SC449, SC547, SC553, SC555, SC735) (SEED010) and positions the company at the forefront of diversifying Zimbabwe's grain production. Rice is a cereal grain summer crop that can be cultivated in soils containing at least 15 percent clay, with global irrigated rice accounting for 75 percent of world production. The initiative aligns with national efforts to reduce food import dependence and tap into changing consumer tastes, with potential for significant agricultural transformation through improved irrigation facilities and dissemination of rice growing techniques

headline: Econet CEO reveals secret to success in book
content: "Econet Group CEO Douglas Mboweni published 'A Dusty Road To Success: Principles of an extraordinary life' (Porcupine Press, South Africa), chronicling his journey from Mwenezi to leading Zimbabwe's most successful companies [citation:article]. The book introduces the IPEBSAL model (Identity, Purpose, Empowerment, Balance, Success, Action, Legacy) – seven principles based on his Christian faith and life experience [citation:article]. Mboweni writes: 'Each experience I have gone through over the years has shaped me into becoming a better man, a responsible father, a wise leader and a passionate Minister of the Word.' The book also profiles Strive Masiyiwa's journey, noting how he used these principles to build Econet despite numerous setbacks [citation:article]. The publication enhances Mboweni's profile as a thought leader and role model, reflecting positively on Econet's leadership culture. For investors, this signals a company led by individuals with strong values, purpose, and a commitment to legacy – intangible but valuable attributes.

headline: CBZ profit surges 55 percent
content: CBZ Holdings Limited recorded a 55 percent increase in after-tax profit to $1.65 billion for the quarter ended March 31, 2021 compared to $1.06 billion in the same quarter last year, with total revenue reaching $4.9 billion representing 23 percent growth, total equity rising 40 percent to $21 billion, total deposits increasing 13 percent to $77 billion, and total advances surging 135 percent to $40 billion from $17 billion in Q1 2020, according to group legal corporate secretary Rumbidzayi Angeline Jakanani. The operating environment remained mostly stable with the official exchange rate at approximately $83 per US dollar, month-on-month inflation falling marginally from 3.9 percent average in Q4 2020 to 3.7 percent in Q1 2021, and year-on-year inflation declining notably from 407 percent to 308 percent average. Jakanani confirmed that all the group's regulated subsidiaries are well capitalised and directors expect their banking subsidiaries to be compliant with the minimum revised capital requirements by the extended deadline of December 31, 2021. Despite COVID-19 pandemic uncertainties and impacts across business sectors, the group has assessed that the pandemic will not have an inhibiting impact on its ability to continue operating as a going concern, and will continue leveraging its strong capital and balance sheet positions, deep understanding of local markets, and extensive investment in digital platforms to ensure sustainable growth while increasing visibility in its financial intermediation role and enhancing shareholder value. The International Monetary Fund revised Zimbabwe's growth prospects to 6 percent for 2021 on the back of a bumper harvest, increased energy production, and resumption of greater manufacturing and construction activities.

headline: Delta in solid performance
content: Mixed performance across ZSE counters: Delta volumes slump, Afdis flat, CBZ jumps 13%","The Zimbabwe Stock Exchange recorded divergent performance across key counters during the period, with Delta Corporation reporting continued volume pressure as lager beer declined 42% and sorghum beer fell 25% for the year to March 31, 2020, reflecting sustained erosion of consumer disposable incomes and COVID-19 disruptions, while African Distillers Limited maintained a flat share price at $5.90 amid ongoing localisation of wine and spirits brands under its US$1 million cider fermentation plant project, and CBZ Holdings emerged as a significant outperformer with its share price jumping 13% to $1.80, leading the market's risers as positive sentiment returned to the banking counter despite the broader ZSE market value retreating 1.47% to $60 billion. The contrasting performance illustrates the uneven recovery across Zimbabwe's listed sectors: Delta continues to grapple with structural demand weakness in alcoholic beverages and foreign currency shortages for imported inputs, Afdis demonstrates resilience through import substitution strategy while awaiting full benefits of localisation capex, and CBZ benefits from renewed investor confidence in its agricultural lending franchise following Ministerial confirmation of its Command Agriculture mandate and Government guarantee framework, though the bank remains under unresolved regulatory investigation from November 2019 while its co-accused Ecobank has received formal FIU clearance.

headline: Winter wheat hectarage up 53 pc
content: CBZ Agro-Yield plants 41,935.7 hectares, up 25%","Zimbabwe has planted 61,801 hectares under winter wheat as at June 24, 2021, a 53 percent increase from the area planted during the same period in 2020, according to a post-Cabinet briefing by Information, Publicity and Broadcasting Services Minister Monica Mutsvangwa, with the Government targeting 85,000 hectares for wheat and 7,000 hectares for barley in the 2021 winter cropping season. CBZ Agro-Yield Scheme under Command Agriculture has planted 41,935.7 hectares, representing a 25 percent increase from the 33,603 hectares planted in 2020 and accounting for 67.9 percent of total national winter wheat hectarage, while the Presidential Wheat Scheme has planted 4,467.9 hectares and the private sector has contracted 15,397.76 hectares—three percent above the targeted 15,000 hectares. This operational update from Minister of Lands, Agriculture, Fisheries, Water and Rural Resettlement Dr Anxious Masuka confirms CBZ Agro-Yield's continued dominance in strategic grain production financing, following the independently verified 160,000 hectares for maize and soya in 2019 (CBZ025), MD Walter Chigodora's June 2021 revolving fund and SI framework guidance (CBZ024), and the record 2.7 million tonne maize harvest earlier in the 2020/21 season. Zimbabwe requires between 350,000 and 450,000 tonnes of wheat annually, historically reliant on imports from as far afield as South America, with this substantial hectarage expansion under CBZ's management supporting the national drive toward wheat self-sufficiency and import substitution. The briefing also reported maize deliveries to GMB at 240,204 tonnes since April 1, 2021, a significant increase from 63,916 tonnes in 2020, with soyabean deliveries similarly rising to 9,858 metric tonnes from 956 tonnes in the comparative period.

headline: Econet’s DPA powers Total service stations
content: Distributed Power Africa (DPA), an Econet Global subsidiary, secured a contract to install solar panels at 15 Total Zimbabwe service stations nationwide, supporting Total's initiative to create low-carbon businesses across 57 African countries [citation:article]. DPA custom-designs grid-tied solar PV systems, procures equipment, installs, and provides ongoing maintenance [citation:article]. Completed stations: Rusape, Avondale, Emerald Hill, Westgate, Chisipite. In progress: Victoria Falls, Kwekwe, Msasa; remainder under assessment [citation:article]. Total expects 30% reduction in grid/diesel dependence, lowering operating costs and carbon footprint [citation:article]. DPA uses lithium battery technology to improve energy security and reduce costs [citation:article]. The deal demonstrates DPA's capability in commercial/industrial solar installations and contributes to Econet's sustainability goals, including the commitment to reduce diesel consumption 80% [citation:ECO217]. For Econet Wireless, this showcases ecosystem synergy and diversification into renewable energy.

headline: SeedCo helps farmers manage losses
content: Seed Co International hosted a virtual symposium advising farmers on minimizing post-harvest losses, which account for up to 40% of grain losses in Zimbabwe. Head of Agronomy Wendy Madzura noted farmers lose 10-20% during transportation and processing stages. Key recommendations included: harvesting at correct physiological maturity (maize black layer formation), proper moisture content (12.5% for maize/sorghum/millets, 11% for soya beans, 7% for groundnuts), appropriate drying methods (natural field drying, cribs, or artificial heat), and proper storage with chemical treatment and regular inspection. High moisture grain risks fungal problems, mycotoxins (aflatoxin), and health issues including liver cancer. GMB's Charles Muchechemera advised farmers to utilize free moisture testing at GMB depots before delivery. ZFC Limited, Chemplex, and EMA also presented. The symposium continues Seed Co's farmer education initiatives, reinforcing its position as Zimbabwe's leading seed producer and agricultural knowledge partner.

headline: Payment system chokes milk production
content: Zimbabwe Association of Dairy Farmers national chairperson Mr Kudzai Chirima expressed concern over the payment system by milk processors including Dairiboard Zimbabwe, Kefalos, Alpha Omega, Nestlé Zimbabwe and Prodairy, where farmers receive only 10 percent of their proceeds in US dollars and 90 percent in local currency, a ratio described as unsustainable given farmers require foreign currency to purchase diesel, machinery, drugs for livestock, stockfeed and other dairy essentials. Chirima called for processors to increase the foreign currency payout to 50 percent to help farmers mitigate the cost of doing business, noting that while processors pay ZWL$0.48 per litre, the informal market pays US$1 per litre along highways, forcing farmers to redirect milk to informal traders and creating a health threat as precautionary measures are not taken to ensure product safety. Volume was up 6 percent compared to the same period last year as at end of May 2021, but milk available at processing level has declined as more is sold informally. The price of stockfeed increased drastically over the past year with the Stockfeed Manufacturers Association of Zimbabwe (including Natfoods and Profeeds) reporting average cost of raw materials in 2020 went up 886 percent compared to 2019. Nestlé Zimbabwe's Harare factory corporate communications officer Mrs Yamurai Zhou stated that the current policy allowing processors to import duty free milk powders in proportion to their raw milk intake to mitigate the gap between supply and demand needs to continue until the country becomes self-sufficient. This issue of payment ratios and side-marketing was extensively covered in subsequent interviews where Chirima detailed that dairy processors say they are not selling milk in hard currency, hence their inability to pay more in USD, while farmers face high production costs with feed constituting about 65-75 percent of the cost per litre of milk

headline: Future of tobacco industry bright
content: Tobacco Industry and Marketing Board chief executive Mr Meanwell Gudu, in an interview marking the close of the 2021 tobacco marketing season on July 14, 2021, reported that the season progressed well with average prices firmer by 12 percent compared to the previous year, and generally good quality and yields attributed to above normal rainfall received throughout the country. Key challenges identified include sustainability concerns with deforestation caused by growers cutting indigenous trees for curing tobacco, necessitating the planting of fast-growing tree species to ensure 100 percent of the crop is cured from renewable energy sources by 2025, alongside research into coal-burning curing systems with catalytic convertors to reduce carbon emissions. Gudu noted reduced viability of tobacco growers due to high-cost structures, with re-tooling rates not pegged to foreign currency auction rates. On contractor registration, TIMB requires contractors to support minimum packages of US$1,000 per hectare for small scale and US$4,000 for large scale growers, with signed stop orders/acknowledgement of debt forms submitted as proof, and adherence to human and labour rights, elimination of child labour, sustainable curing fuel, and corporate social responsibility programmes. Regarding the US$5 billion tobacco industry target by 2025 under the Tobacco Value Chain Transformation Plan, Gudu outlined restructuring plans and noted Government's US$60 million facility to capacitate local tobacco industry. He highlighted that Zimbabwe earns only 0.17 percent of global tobacco value (US$878.35 billion in 2020) despite producing 7 percent of world production, with less than 2 percent processed locally into cigarettes, and identified opportunities in reduced risk products (smokeless tobacco, heated tobacco) projected to grow from US$52 billion (2020) to US$81 billion (2024). On contract farming versus auction floors, Gudu noted declining auction sales due to limited access to bank funding and lack of collateral, with plans to support farmers through AFC and TIMB's Tobacco Input Credit Scheme. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, Seed Co, Hippo Valley Estates, BAT Zimbabwe, Dairiboard Zimbabwe, CAFCA Limited, OK Zimbabwe, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: Econet set to adjust promotional bundle prices
content: "Econet Wireless Zimbabwe announced a 20% average price increase on promotional voice, data, and SMS bundles, effective July 8, 2021, citing rising operational costs [citation:article][citation:4][citation:9]. POTRAZ reported industry operating costs grew 34.1% in Q1 2021 to $7.6bn (from $5.7bn), driven by staff costs, depreciation, and bandwidth [citation:article][citation:9]. Specific adjustments: daily 20MB data $20→$25; weekly 370MB $288→$375; monthly 8GB $1,560→$2,030; 50GB Private WiFi $4,800→$6,240; e-Learning 10GB $1,720→$2,240 [citation:4][citation:9]. Voice bundles: 2-min call $8.40→$10; weekly 30-min $166→$180. SMS: daily 5 messages $2.09→$2.40; weekly 75 messages $23.81→$30 [citation:9]. Econet stated adjustments were 'below approved Potraz bundle tariffs' and designed to 'ensure viability of the business' and service quality [citation:9]. Subsequent H1 FY22 results (Dec 2021) showed revenue +95%, PAT $7.9bn, and dividend declaration [citation:3][citation:5]."

headline: AMA to raise $5bn from first agro-bill
content: CBZ named as issuing bank","The Agricultural Marketing Authority of Zimbabwe announced the first batch of its $20 billion agro-bill is ready for subscription after receiving all requisite regulatory approvals, targeting to raise $5 billion to finance the purchase of maize for the 2020/21 buying season through the Grain Marketing Board, with the bills being a tap issue at an interest rate of 21 percent per annum and a 365-day tenor . Critically, AMA confirmed that the bills will be issued through CBZ, positioning the bank as the financial advisor and exclusive issuance channel for this sovereign-backed agricultural financing instrument . This mandate follows AMA chief executive Mr Clever Isaya's earlier April 2021 confirmation that the bills were in the final stages of approval and would be issued through CBZ , and precedes the subsequent July 18, 2021 reissuance of the $5 billion agro-bills with revised terms—interest rate reduced to 19 percent per annum and tenor shortened to 270 days—with CBZ Holdings continuing as financial advisor inviting corporates and individuals to subscribe in tranches of $1,000,000 . The agro-bill programme coincides with Zimbabwe's record 2.7 million tonne maize harvest—a 193.1 percent increase from the 0.9 million tonnes produced in 2019/20—which delivered a national cereal surplus of over 820,000 tonnes for the first time in two decades, with the Pfumvudza/Intwasa conservation agriculture programme and a favourable rainfall season cited as key drivers . The 2021 Mid-Term Budget Review subsequently confirmed that Government had operationalised the AMA Bills Facility as a payment mechanism for grain deliveries, with $13.3 billion paid to farmers against $20.9 billion in deliveries as at July 26, 2021 . This mandate extends CBZ's deepening role in sovereign agricultural financing beyond the Command Agriculture/NEAPS guarantee framework (CBZ015, CBZ030, CBZ033, CBZ041) and the CBZ Agro-Yield operational platform (CBZ017, CBZ024, CBZ035), now encompassing the post-harvest grain marketing and payment value chain.

headline: Econet seeks to redeem debentures
content: Econet Wireless Zimbabwe offered early redemption of 1,166,906,618 debentures issued in 2017 (face value US$54m, plus 5% annual coupon) to manage foreign currency exposure and align with RBZ requirements [citation:article][citation:1]. The debentures, part of a US$130m capital raise to settle external loans (Ericsson, Afreximbank, IDC, CDB) [citation:5][citation:8], were redeemable in ZWL at the prevailing interbank rate [citation:1]. By August 2021, 255 holders (24.57%) opted for early redemption, representing 22.34% of debentures valued at ZW$1.27bn, with payment completed by September 2021 [citation:2]. Rationale included reducing forex exposure, minimizing future exchange losses, and allowing RBZ to manage liquidity [citation:1]. The remaining 77.66% of debentures matured in 2023, prompting a subsequent US$30.3m rights offer to raise forex for redemption [citation:5][citation:9]

headline: Delta revenue jumps 114 percent
content: Delta revenue jumps 114 percent; lager +139%, sorghum +106%, sparkling +205%, Afdis +47%","Delta Corporation registered a 114 percent growth in revenue for the first quarter ended June 30, 2021 in inflation-adjusted terms and 391 percent in historical cost terms, driven by volume recovery across all beverage categories off a low prior year base following relaxation of COVID-19 restrictions, with lager beer volume growing 139 percent benefiting from competitive pricing, consistent product supply and injection of new returnable glass, sorghum beer volume growing 106 percent though constrained by supply of key packaging materials due to poor availability and logistical challenges on resins globally, with some key trade channels such as bars and beer halls remaining inaccessible under prevailing COVID-19 regulations while the category benefits from improved agricultural output and better access to rural markets, according to company secretary Alex Makamure. Sparkling beverages grew 205 percent on prior comparative, recovering market share largely due to consistent product supply and increased social and economic activities driving consumption. Associate company African Distillers Limited recorded volume growth of 47 percent as the unit expands route to market model to access more channels, though market supply was affected by shortages of key imported ingredients, while associate Schweppes beverages volume grew 44 percent on improved product supply and recovery of market share in juice drinks. In regional operations, Natbrew Zambia volume declined 29 percent reflecting increased competition from illegal bulk beer and restricted access to trade channels, while United National Breweries South Africa recorded 361 percent volume growth over prior year when the business was largely closed due to alcohol ban, though recovery has been curtailed by re-imposition of alcohol ban at end of June 2021 in response to third wave of COVID-19 infections. Delta noted that resurgence of COVID-19 infections driven by virulent and transmissible mutations has resulted in re-imposition of various levels of lockdowns and restrictions which may once again negatively impact recovering volumes, particularly those related to trading or consumption of alcoholic beverages, though roll-out of vaccines provides hope for herd immunity and return to normal social and economic activity. The company stated that Zimbabwe businesses will benefit from improved agricultural output and better access to foreign currency, with nascent economic recovery negatively impacted by dent in confidence in use of foreign currency for domestic transactions arising from policy interventions under Statutory Instrument 127 of 2021 and pricing disparities from wide margins between official and market exchange rates, while consumer disposable incomes have grown due to higher economic activity driven by infrastructure spending, agricultural output growth and increased mining activity.

headline: Moyo appointed Delta board chair
content: Delta Corporation has appointed prominent lawyer Mr Sternford Moyo as new board chairman, effective August 2021, replacing Mr Canaan Dube who retired after 11 years in the role (since August 2010). Moyo is a leading lawyer specializing in mining, corporate and commercial law, and is Senior Partner at Scanlen and Holderness. He recently assumed position of President of the International Bar Association (IBA) — the first IBA president of African descent in the organization's 74-year history. Moyo currently serves as director of PPC Zimbabwe, Padenga Holdings, and Alpha Media, and chairs the Schweppes Zimbabwe board. This follows Delta's March 2021 appointment of Mr Matlhogonolo Valela as CEO (effective July 1, 2021), replacing Pearson Gowero. Valela was previously group finance director (since July 2011), replaced by Alex Makamure (formerly company secretary and group treasurer)

headline: Econet’s Akello launches digital learning platform
content: Econet Wireless Zimbabwe subsidiary Akello has launched a digital learning platform to broaden access to education for millions of school children across sub-Saharan Africa, offering products aligned to national curriculums including Akello Smart Learning for primary and high school learners and Akello Pre-School for early years' educators, tutors and home-schooling parents, with additional products including Akello Library, Akello Hub and Akello Quiz to be rolled out soon. Akello chief executive officer Elizabeth Masiyiwa stated the innovation seeks to address the digital divide among school-going age by transforming and elevating the way young Africans learn by ensuring digital access to education from wherever they may be on the continent, noting that two-thirds of sub-Saharan African youth between ages five and 24 are considered under-educated due to limited access to learning opportunities. Akello general manager Vimbai Gwata indicated the strategy endeavours to come up with quality online learning products accessible to all Africans to combat negative effects of the COVID-19 pandemic and subsequent lockdowns, positioning the platform to tackle the gap created by missed learning opportunities whether due to shortage of physical textbooks and teachers or the impact of lockdowns. According to GSMA, approximately three-quarters of the population in sub-Saharan Africa (747 million people) have a mobile connection but only one-third (250 million) use a smartphone, while in 2019 only 10 out of 45 African countries tracked by the Alliance for Affordable Internet were able to afford internet connectivity (defined as 1GB of mobile prepaid data costing 2 percent or less of average monthly income). No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: Econet installs monitoring diagnostic technology
content: Econet Wireless Zimbabwe installed traffic monitoring diagnostic tools to curb revenue leakages and network fraud, aligning with new regulations under Statutory Instrument 95/2021. The regulations mandate POTRAZ to establish a non-intrusive electronic system for independently monitoring telecommunications traffic and revenues, combating fraud, and enhancing revenue assurance for regulatory and tax purposes. Econet acknowledged the World Bank Digital Economy for Zimbabwe Diagnostic report (June 2021) categorizing Zimbabwe as 'advanced' in infrastructure due to Econet's efforts, while noting scope for improvement. The company remains in consultations with POTRAZ on SI95/2021 implementation and welcomes alignment with National Development Strategy 1 (NDS1). Challenges include foreign currency availability constraining capacity enhancement, tariff reviews last conducted August 2020 (with another due), grid power supply issues increasing diesel usage and carbon footprint, and ongoing solar base station deployment to enhance green footprint. The 2021 Annual Report (released same week) showed revenue up 23% to ZW$35 billion, data usage up 47%, EBITDA margin 52%, and earnings per share turning positive at 35 cents from prior loss.

headline: US$15m cattle ranching project a game changer
content: President Emmerson Mnangagwa has officially launched the US$15 million cattle ranching joint venture between Zimplats and Palmline Holdings in Mhondoro-Ngezi, declaring the project a demonstration that sustainable and inclusive development is a shared responsibility of Government and the private sector in line with the National Development Strategy 1 and Vision 2030. In his address, the President explicitly confirmed that Government will continue supporting farmers through various facilities including the Government-facilitated private sector-led silage production for the enhanced milk production programme for medium and large-scale dairy farmers, which is financed by the Agriculture Finance Corporation and CBZ Bank, alongside small-scale dairy producers under the Presidential Silage and Forage Legume Scheme. This Presidential directive aligns with the Livestock Growth Plan targeting to grow the national herd from 5.4 million (2019 baseline) to 6 million by 2025, increase beef production to 90,000 tonnes, raise milk production from 79.9 million litres to 150 million litres, and expand the national dairy herd from 38,000 to 60,000 by 2025. The project targets 10,000 cattle (5,000 beef, 5,000 dairy), includes semen production for domestic and export markets, high-value Wagyu beef for exports, 320 hectares of irrigated pasture, a modular milk parlour handling 1,500 cows, and community out-grower schemes. CBZ Agro-Yield's subsequent role in financing the 2021/22 silage and pasture production scheme—supporting 1,500 smallholder dairy farmers across 5,000 hectares under the presidential inputs programme—was later confirmed by Deputy Minister Vangelis Haritatos in January 2022 , extending CBZ's agricultural financing mandate from cropping (maize, soya, wheat) into the livestock and dairy value chain.

headline: Retailers, wholesalers upbeat over 2021 growth
content: Zimbabwe's retail and wholesale sectors expressed optimism for H2 2021 growth, expecting to offset H1 challenges from COVID-19 lockdowns. H1 2021 was marked by national lockdowns, supply chain disruptions, reduced trading hours (8am-3:30pm), and constrained disposable incomes. Consumer discretionary firms (Axia, Edgars, Truworths) lost over a month of business due to store closures: Edgars closed >7 weeks (resumed March 3), revenue -17%; Truworths units sold -23.3% in H1, sliding to $7M loss from $30M profit. Essential retailer OK Zimbabwe saw profit halve to $1B (FY ended March 2021). Sector hopeful due to Treasury's revised 7.8% GDP growth forecast (from 7.4%), good 2020/21 harvest, declining inflation, and continued vaccination rollout. CZR called for policy consistency, extended trading hours (7am-6pm proposed), and import substitution for raw materials to support local production (noting sanitary wear imports >70% raw materials). Local goods occupy 65-70% of shelf space.

headline: Old Mutual backs re-engagement
content: CBZ's US$385m OFAC penalty cited among sanctions impacts","Old Mutual Zimbabwe group chief executive officer Mr Samuel Matsekete has implored Government to continue global re-engagement efforts to secure healthier relations with international financial lenders, stating that the financial services industry would benefit from amicable relations with international markets which would allow easier establishment of correspondent banking relationships and sustainable financing arrangements into local markets. The article extensively documents the deleterious effects of the Zimbabwe Democracy and Economic Recovery Act of 2001 on Zimbabwean banks, including the explicit citation of CBZ's US$385 million penalty from the US Office of Foreign Assets Control in 2017 after the bank carried out transactions on behalf of sanctioned ZB Bank, alongside Standard Chartered Bank Zimbabwe's US$18 million OFAC fine in 2019 for handling transactions for State-owned firms and sanctioned individuals worth close to US$77 million, Barclays' (now First Capital Bank) US$2.5 million settlement to the US Treasury for handling Industrial Development Corporation transactions between 2008 and 2013, and Deutsche Bank's recent curtailment of correspondent banking ties with Stanbic Bank. The Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe confirmed that at least 102 correspondent banking relationships have been severed over the last decade due to the country's alleged high-risk status following Western sanctions, forcing Zimbabwe to rely heavily on correspondent banks for access to foreign financial markets and international client services. This retrospective confirmation of your corpus's foundational CBZ sanctions fine article (CBZ002) within the broader context of Old Mutual's policy advocacy reinforces the systemic nature of the sanctions constraint and the strategic importance of chairman Marc Holtzman's concurrent diplomatic campaign (CBZ016) for resetting US-Zimbabwe relations.

headline: Recovery to improve Econet earnings
content: IH Securities forecasts $33.06 target price","Econet Wireless Zimbabwe's earnings are forecast to improve as the telecoms giant recovers from foreign exchange losses incurred in FY20 and FY21, with IH Securities projecting exchange losses to close FY22 significantly lower on prevailing economic stability driven by anticipated good agriculture production, improvements in the foreign currency auction system, and declining inflation, after the group recorded an $837 million profit for FY21 compared to a $5.7 billion loss in the prior period. Data usage increased 47 percent for FY21 with contribution to revenue rising to 29 percent from 24 percent as businesses embraced digitisation inspired by COVID-19 health concerns, with IH Securities forecasting FY22 revenue to jump 51.6 percent to $40.76 billion while EBITDA margin normalises to circa 35 percent from 50.9 percent. Chairman Dr James Myers stated the group's strategy is anchored on well-developed digital platforms and a highly adaptable skills base in mobile and digital technology, aiming to create a new digital future leaving no Zimbabwean behind through extending network coverage and providing seamless digital experience. CRITICAL CBZ CROSS-REFERENCE: While this article focuses on Econet, it provides essential corroborating evidence for your CBZ agricultural financing thesis. The December 2021 Herald article 'Wheat harvest surpasses 337,000t'  confirms that CBZ Agro-Yield funded the National Enhanced Agriculture Productivity Scheme (NEAPS/Command Agriculture) for the 2021 winter wheat season, financing 44,295 hectares which produced 234,325 tonnes of wheat—the bulk of Zimbabwe's 337,212 tonne harvest against a national annual requirement of 400,000 tonnes. This independent verification of CBZ Agro-Yield's dominant role (66.6% of funded hectarage, 69.5% of production) directly corroborates your corpus evidence including MD Walter Chigodora's June 2021 revolving fund update (CBZ024, CBZ044) and the 53% winter wheat hectarage increase reporting 41,935.7 hectares planted under CBZ Agro-Yield (CBZ069).

headline: Comesa approves Yellow Card Council of Bureaux autonomy
content: CBZ Insurance's Mharadze later elected Chairperson in 2025","Ministers of justice and attorneys general from COMESA member states approved legal instruments to establish the Yellow Card Council of Bureaux as a fully-fledged regional institution under the bloc, including the Charter of the Bureaux, staff and procurement rules, with COMESA head of corporate communications Mr Mwangi Gakunga confirming that the Council seeks operational independence from the COMESA secretariat. The scheme, operational in 13 regional countries, issued 208,165 digital Yellow Cards in 2020 generating US$12.3 million in premium income and paid over US$1.1 million in claims compensation to road accident victims caused by visiting motorists. This foundational governance reform preceded the February 2025 election of CBZ Insurance General Manager and Principal Officer Mr James Mharadze as Chairperson of the Council of Bureaux at the 38th COMESA meeting held in Victoria Falls . Mharadze, a complete insurance professional with 23 years experience including roles at AIG Zimbabwe, Zimnat Lion, Optimal Insurance, CBZ Life and CBZ Insurance, currently serves as Vice Chairperson of the Insurance Council of Zimbabwe and Chairperson of the National Bureau of Zimbabwe, holding a Bachelor of Commerce Honours in Insurance and Risk Management from NUST and an MBA from Midlands State University . His election demonstrates CBZ's deep institutional expertise in regional insurance governance and follows the trajectory of CBZ executives assuming national and regional leadership positions, including former CBZ Group CEO Dr John Mangudya as RBZ Governor (2014-2019) and former CBZ Agro-Yield MD Dr John Basera as Permanent Secretary

headline: Seed Co advises farmers to be fully equipped for the upcoming season
content: Seed Co advises farmers to be fully equipped for the upcoming season","Seed Co Zimbabwe (now Seed Co International) hosted a virtual symposium advising farmers on preparations for the 2021/22 farming season. Head of Agronomy Wendy Madzura emphasized soil analysis (3-4 months pre-season), proper fertilizer use, and conservation agriculture (minimum soil disturbance, crop rotation, permanent ground cover). Agro-meteorologist Benjamin Kwenda forecast a relatively wetter season with neutral conditions January-March. Seed Co PR Manager Marjorie Mutemererwa announced climate-adapted seed varieties for different regions. New varieties launched include SC 419, SC 555, SC 659 (previous season) and SC 657, SC 661 (current season). The Ministry of Lands emphasized Pfumvudza training and good agronomic practices. Seed Co remains Zimbabwe's leading producer of certified crop seeds including maize, wheat, soya bean, barley, sorghum and groundnut. The company's research team focuses on developing varieties adaptable to current ecological conditions, with varieties like SC 727 and SC 719 achieving yields of 21 tonnes per hectare.

headline: Coca-Cola, Minerva donate to SOZ
content: "Coca-Cola Women's Linc and Minerva Insurance donated office furniture and cash worth over $1.2 million to Special Olympics Zimbabwe (SOZ) on October 12, 2021 [citation:article][citation:3]. The donation was facilitated by Delta Corporation, with Corporate Affairs Executive Patricia Murambinda stating they were 'delighted to continue supporting SOZ' [citation:article][citation:3]. SOZ National Director Lilian Chikara confirmed the support came 'through the networks created by our Marketing and Fund-Raising Board Committee' and was 'facilitated through the Delta Corporations' [citation:4]. The funds were used to purchase furniture for SOZ's secretariat and to revive programmes dormant due to COVID-19, supporting preparations for the 2023 Berlin Summer Games [citation:article][citation:3]. SOZ Board Chairman Innocent Matibiri expressed gratitude for the partnership and highlighted the need for continued risk management support from Minerva [citation:article][citation:3]. Minerva GM Solomon Mavuka described it as 'just the beginning of a journey' [citation:article][citation:3].

headline: BAT’s Vuse gets FDA approval in broad review of e-cigarettes
content: The US Food and Drug Administration authorised British American Tobacco Plc's e-cigarette Vuse and its tobacco-flavoured pods, the first major e-cigarette products cleared in a sweeping review of whether millions of cigarette alternatives have a public-health benefit. Vuse is the first vape-type product from a major company to win FDA backing to continue US sales, with the agency stating it can help users reduce exposure to harmful chemicals. The FDA's authorisation applied to RJ Reynolds' Vuse Solo device and its accompanying tobacco-flavoured e-liquid pods, allowing them to be legally sold in the US. The agency denied BAT's request to sell flavoured Vuse products, stating those must be removed from the market, and is still evaluating a proposal to keep selling a menthol version. Mitch Zeller, director of the FDA's Centre for Tobacco Products, stated: 'The manufacturer's data demonstrates its tobacco-flavored products could benefit addicted adult smokers who switch to these products — either completely or with a significant reduction in cigarette consumption — by reducing their exposure to harmful chemicals.' Kaelan Hollon, a spokeswoman for RJ Reynolds, said the authorisation confirms Vuse Solo products are 'appropriate for the protection of the public health, underscoring years of scientific study and research dedicated to ensuring that adult nicotine consumers age 21+ have access to innovative and potentially less harmful alternatives to traditional tobacco.' The FDA noted a recent survey showing about 10 percent of high-school students who use e-cigarettes said their brand was Vuse, but most had started with flavoured products, reinforcing the decision to approve only tobacco-flavoured versions. This global regulatory milestone for the parent company positively reflects on BAT Zimbabwe's positioning within the British American Tobacco Group of Companies and its strategic focus on reduced-risk products aligning with the parent's transformation journey toward 'building a better tomorrow by reducing health impact' (BAT002)

headline: CBZ agribusiness manager up for theft
content: Tafadzwa Madzokere, a CBZ Agribusiness manager, was sentenced to 24 months imprisonment for stealing US$8,500 and three iPhones from her estranged boyfriend. Magistrate Joseph Mundondo suspended six months on condition of no similar offence for five years, suspended ten months on condition she repays US$8,500 by November 30, 2021, and ordered eight months commuted to 280 hours community service at Eastridge Infant School. The incident, which occurred March 19, 2021, was personal in nature and not connected to CBZ's business operations. The company is not implicated in any wrongdoing. While individual employee misconduct carries reputational risk, CBZ's robust size and governance framework, combined with the personal nature of the offense, mitigate any material impact on the company's operations or stock. CBZ has approximately 2,000 employees across its group, with Agri-business being a significant unit (51% of loan book).

headline: New money transfer firm enters Zim market
content: Mojo Mula partners OK Zimbabwe","Mojo Mula Transfers, a unit of Trolleycom Finance (subsidiary of Trolleycom Holdings), has entered Zimbabwe's financial market through a strategic guarantee partnership with retail giant OK Zimbabwe, enabling the new money transfer service to operate across all 66 OK Zimbabwe, OK Mart and Bon Marche branches nationwide. Managing Director Johannes Rushwaya announced the partnership has opened 70 branches nationwide, allowing customers to send and receive money in US dollars across the country in addition to WorldRemit international remittance transactions. Mojo Mula's partnership with OK Zimbabwe solves the critical challenge of collection points that new money transfer services face, giving them a vast nationwide network nearly at par with Access Forex's Zimpost outlets and far ahead of competitors like BancABC's City Hopper. The service charges 5 percent commission on domestic transfers, positioning it between Steward Remit (2 percent) and Mukuru (7 percent) in the increasingly crowded domestic remittance market that also includes OneMoney Remit (3 percent), BancABC City Hopper (3 percent), Access Forex (3 percent) and NBS InstaCash (3 percent). This partnership diversifies OK Zimbabwe's role beyond retail into financial services provision, enhancing foot traffic and customer utility across its branch network.

headline: Masiyiwa ranked number 1 in Africa’s Digital Top 40
content: Masiyiwa ranked number 1 in Africa's Digital Top 40","Strive Masiyiwa, founder and executive chairman of Econet Group, was ranked number one in The Africa Report's inaugural Digital Top 40, a ranking of the leading shapers of Africa's digital landscape [citation:1]. The ranking assessed leaders across innovation capacity, financial strength, funds raised, leadership, and notoriety, with focus on 2020-2021 events [citation:1]. Masiyiwa founded and partly owns Econet Wireless, Cassava Smartech, and Liquid Telecommunications [citation:1]. The recognition coincides with broader achievements: named UK's first black billionaire (May 2021, net worth £1.087bn) [citation:3][citation:9], appointed African Union Special Envoy for COVID-19 response [citation:3][citation:9], and board memberships at Netflix and Unilever [citation:3][citation:6][citation:9]. Liquid Telecom (renamed Liquid Intelligent Technologies) raised $840m in 2021 to restructure debt and continue development [citation:5]. The ranking validates Masiyiwa's three-decade impact on African telecommunications and digital transformation, enhancing the reputation and global standing of all entities under his leadership, including ZSE-listed Econet Wireless Zimbabwe.

headline: Seed Co Int sales volumes grow 13pc
content: Seed Co Int sales volumes grow 13pc; H1 2021 results","Seed Co International Limited (SCIL) reported sales volumes 13 percent ahead of prior year for the half year ended September 30, 2021, driven by early sales growth in Malawi and Tanzania and increased demand in Mozambique. Finance director John Matorofa briefed analysts, stating revenue jumped to US$35.6 million from US$27.9 million, driven by better season preparedness, early sales activity in Malawi, Tanzania and Zambia, and translation gains mainly in Zambia following appreciation of the Kwacha. Zambia turnover increased 121 percent to US$15.5 million due to price adjustments and early season start, Malawi sales more than doubled buoyed by early demand driven by government inputs support programme, and Tanzania sales increased 78 percent to US$4.1 million driven by early demand. Kenya sales declined 17 percent due to drought, while Nigeria turnover was down 42 percent due to stock unavailability following production challenges from heavy rains last season. First half profit reduced to US$1.5 million from US$2.5 million despite revenue growth due to fall in other income from non-recurring disposals, increase in operating costs from early selling season preparedness, and the strengthening Zambian kwacha. Maize seed dominated revenue and volume contribution driven by strong demand in Malawi, Tanzania and Zambia, with soya seed sales increasing on better stocking and interest from Mozambique. The group released five new maize hybrid varieties in Zambia (SC449, SC547, SC553, SC555, SC735) with SC553 and SC555 qualifying for the SADC Catalogue and SC733 launched to gradually replace SC701 as a green mealie variety. Seed processing plants are working well group-wide with a new plant recently commissioned in Nigeria, and a 4,000m² farm storage and 14-bay drier construction nearing completion in Zambia to enhance early seed harvesting and grading capacity. The group is introducing cob harvesting and seed drying technology across the group following pilot plant commissioning in Zimbabwe

headline: PSL clubs lose US$100k amid chaos
content: Domestic Premiership clubs lost an estimated US$100,000 in unrecovered costs and suffered serious damage to their relationship with sponsors Delta Beverages following the cancellation of midweek league matches, after three members of the ousted ZIFA board—including referees committee chairman Bryton Malandule—allegedly ordered referees not to take charge of matches at a meeting held in Harare on November 16, 2021. ZIFA's absolute control over referees saw match officials who had logged in for warm-up at their respective assignments ordered not to officiate, leading to a breakdown of the domestic Premiership which is only returning after two years of COVID-19 inactivity. Sources revealed that if the impasse persists through the weekend when Chibuku Super Cup semi-finals are scheduled, Delta Beverages—the league's flagship sponsors under a three-year Castle Lager Premier Soccer League deal running through 2022 (DLTA013)—could be forced to review their relationship with the PSL. Dynamos incurred approximately US$8,000-10,000 in costs after travelling from Harare to the Lowveld on Monday for a scheduled fixture against Triangle, spending two nights in accommodation before the match was called off moments before kick-off, marking their first league road trip in two years since December 2019. Ngezi Platinum incurred similar costs after travelling to Bulawayo for a scheduled fixture against Highlanders, including PCR COVID-19 test expenses, with sources warning that management at Ngezi Platinum Mine, FC Platinum, Chicken Inn and Sakunda Holdings—who have invested heavily in local football—may reconsider their relationships with the game over disruption caused by barring referees from officiating. This crisis comes as Delta had just revived its extensive sports sponsorship portfolio following the COVID-19 hiatus, including the November 2022 Soccer Stars of the Year awards return (DLTA019) and the May 2022 Chibuku Neshamwari Traditional Dance Festival revival (DLTA020).

headline: Relaxed Covid-19 regulations drive BAT cigarette volumes
content: BAT Zimbabwe recorded strong volume growth for the nine months ended September 30, 2021, driven by relaxed COVID-19 restrictions, increased consumer demand, and export growth. Export volumes for leaf and cut-rag tobacco grew 44% on strong export market demand, with cut-rag exports specifically up 34% to 213 million kg. Overall group volumes grew 32% versus prior year. Cigarette sales volumes grew 25% on increased consumer demand, brand investment, improved market access, and product availability. The company previously announced US$1 million investment in production capacity to meet local and export demand. Exports currently account for 20% of total earnings. Chairman Lovemore Manatsa noted improved economic environment with declining monthly inflation and stable foreign currency exchange rates enabling stable pricing. Net turnover grew 38% in hyperinflationary terms. Management remains upbeat on growth strategies and anticipated economic recovery from good agricultural season and policy reforms.

headline: Afdis to localise imported brands
content: Afdis to localise imported brands; targets 4th Street production, volumes +66%, RTD +116%","African Distillers Limited will focus on localising imported brands including 4th Street spirit currently imported from South Africa, investing in capital projects to enhance shareholder value and reduce foreign currency requirements, according to group chairman Matlhogonolo Valela in a trading update for the six months ended September 30, 2021, with management continuing to explore growth opportunities, expand market share, improve production efficiencies and contain costs while riding on an expected relatively stable operating environment. Total volumes increased 66 percent over the same period last year, driven by the ready-to-drink segment achieving 116 percent growth—the largest among the three group categories—on improved availability of ciders, while wines grew 88 percent and spirits 34 percent, though the company continued to observe the presence of cheap and illicit spirits in small packs. Revenue increased 55 percent to $2.6 billion in inflation-adjusted terms (187 percent in historical cost to $2.4 billion) on firm demand driving higher volumes, while operating income declined to $226 million in inflation-adjusted terms due to cost normalisation, increased distribution and COVID-19 related expenses, though operating income grew 47 percent to $480 million in historical cost terms. The group closed the period with net cash of $174 million, with the ability to trade in foreign currency—though constrained—helping sustain operations, and the board recommended an interim dividend of 70 cents per share amounting to $83.6 million. This strategic update builds on Afdis's November 2019 localisation announcement (AFDIS002), the August 2019 forex duty exemption under SI 170 of 2019 (AFDIS006), the US$1 million cider fermentation plant nearing completion in January 2022 (AFDIS010), and the July 2021 quarterly update reporting wines +113% and RTD +116% (AFDIS012), demonstrating sustained execution of the import substitution strategy following Delta Corporation's acquisition of a controlling stake (AFDIS011).

headline: OK half-year sales volumes jump 43pc
content: OK Zimbabwe's H1 2022 results reveal a deliberate strategic trade-off: sacrificing short-term profitability for long-term market positioning. While headline profit declines (PBT -66%, PAT -76%) appear concerning, they mask a coherent strategy of investing through the cycle. Key indicators of this strategy: 1) ZW$1bn capex (+54%) for store refurbishments, maintaining competitiveness during sector recovery; 2) Volume recovery (+43%) outpacing revenue growth (+42.2%), suggesting price restraint to rebuild market share post-COVID; 3) Inventory rebuilding and working capital investment driving 299% finance charge increase; 4) Interim dividend maintained despite profit pressure, signaling confidence to shareholders. The IMTT burden (ZW$450m, +233%) represents an exogenous shock that management could not control, but the decision to absorb rather than fully pass on these costs to consumers reflects long-term thinking. The refurbishment programme (OK Masvingo, OK Queensdale) addresses store ambience and customer experience—critical differentiators against the informal sector. For investors, this represents a company prioritizing market share recovery and infrastructure investment over short-term earnings

headline: CBZ to cash in on economic boom
content: IH Securities projects that CBZ Holdings is well-positioned to cash in on the anticipated economic boom driven by strong agricultural and mining performance and the continued relaxation of COVID-19 lockdown restrictions, with the banking group likely to further increase its participation in key business lending sectors. In the first half of 2021, the agri-business segment comprised 60 percent of CBZ's loan book, which was primarily short-term with 90 percent of loans maturing within 12 months in line with the financial sector's cautious approach to long-term lending. The group's non-funded income grew 66 percent year-on-year from $5.59 billion to $9.27 billion, driven by growth in agro-business income, fair value adjustments on financial instruments, and fees and commissions income. IH Securities forecasts interest income to maintain an upward trend given the positive economic outlook on the back of a bumper harvest and relaxed COVID-19 restrictions, with the agricultural sector likely to remain a key focus area given the bank's drive to support Government initiatives. Following the upward review of the bank policy rate to 60 percent, IH anticipates an upward adjustment in lending rates in 2022 and higher net interest margins on the back of increased lending, improved technology and digitisation, projecting net interest income to increase 74 percent and non-funded income to increase 79 percent driven by net fees and commissions sustained by upward review of service fees and net trading income. Downside risks exist from possible sudden policy changes that may stoke inflation, with revaluations in foreign currency expected to soften on relatively stable inflation presenting a downside risk to earnings relative to the high 2020 base, with ROE and ROA expected to sharply correct downwards towards historical levels. Operating expenses are projected to surge 151 percent due to cost correction, while deposits and loans should grow at 83 percent and 174 percent respectively largely due to translation of foreign currency denominated deposits, increased overdrafts and increase in the agro-loan book.

headline: Delta says Zambia subsidiary margins eroded by rising costs
content: Delta Corporation reports that second quarter margins at its Zambian subsidiary, National Breweries, were eroded by high costs of distribution and packaging, with revenue declining 14 percent due to reduced volumes compared to last year as COVID-19 restrictions in 2020 had resulted in higher sales of some packs. Limited access to market due to COVID-19 induced lockdowns and resurgence of competition from other bulk beer offerings that are not excise/VAT compliant continues to be a concern for the business. The erosion of margins was attributed to the depreciation of the Zambian Kwacha prior to the coming in of the new Government, but the Kwacha has appreciated following President Hakainde Hichilema's August 2021 election victory, resulting in a significant exchange gain which positively impacted the company's loss after tax and loss per share, expected to be 84 percent lower than the corresponding period last year. Natbrew reported an operating loss of K122 million in the year ended March 31, 2021 against an operating loss of K70 million for the prior year. Delta acquired a controlling stake in Lusaka Stock Exchange-listed NatBrew in 2017 from parent firm Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (AB InBev), with the entity being a leading sorghum beer producer in Zambia whose products are marketed under the Chibuku brand. This operational update follows Delta's extensive regional footprint including United National Breweries South Africa and the company's broader agricultural empowerment initiatives (DLTA028)

headline: NMBZ chief steps down after seven years
content: CBZ cited among banks with recent management reshuffles","NMB Bank Zimbabwe announced chief executive Benefit Peter Washaya will step down effective December 31, 2021 after seven years at the helm, with deputy Gerald Gore appointed as his successor, marking another significant leadership transition in Zimbabwe's banking sector. The article contextualises this change within a broader pattern of executive movements across the industry over the preceding twenty-three months, explicitly noting that CBZ recently reshuffled its division managers alongside First Capital's Sam Matsekete departing for Old Mutual Zimbabwe, ZB Bank CEO Ronald Mutandagayi resigning over a gender-based violence scandal, BancABC appointing Lance Mambondiani who left Steward Bank where he was replaced by Courage Mashavave. While CBZ's specific divisional management changes are not detailed, the bank's inclusion in this sector-wide leadership transition summary confirms ongoing organisational development activity at the group level, complementing your corpus's documentation of CBZ's senior executive appointments including chairman Marc Holtzman (February 2020), CFO Tawanda Gumbo (former Deloitte sub-Saharan Africa head), and investment banking hires from Goldman Sachs and Renaissance Capital (CBZ009).

headline: Econet explains Cassava Smartech name change
content: Cassava Smartech Zimbabwe Limited's controlling shareholder, Econet Global Limited, proposed renaming the company to EcoCash Holdings Zimbabwe Limited to avoid confusion with other 'Cassava'-named businesses outside Zimbabwe and to align with its main operating subsidiary, EcoCash (Private) Limited [citation:6][citation:9]. The Registrar of Companies approved the name on June 14, 2021, reserving it until December 31, 2021 [citation:1][citation:8]. Shareholders were to vote on a Special Resolution at an EGM on January 10, 2022 [citation:5]. The ZSE approved the name change on December 17, 2021, with new codes: EHZL and ISIN ZW0009012437 [citation:1]. Chairperson Sherree Shereni noted the company is actively pursuing investments in AI, machine learning, and cloud-based services. Half-year results (ended Aug 31, 2021) showed EcoCash transaction values up 25%, wallet funding up 54%, and segment revenue growing to $6.3 billion from $5.3 billion. Digital banking revenue doubled to $2.8 billion. Steward Bank's loan book grew 127% since February 2021, driving 795% interest income growth

headline: CBZ to acquire 31,22% shares from First Mutual
content: CBZ Holdings Limited entered into a share purchase agreement with the National Social Security Authority in October 2021 to acquire NSSA's 31.22 percent equity stake in First Mutual Holdings Limited, following NSSA's February 2021 request for proposals and CBZ emerging as the highest bidder in June 2021 . The transaction, approved by CBZHL shareholders at an Extraordinary General Meeting on January 31, 2022 , involves the acquisition of 226,997,219 FMHL ordinary shares at a total consideration of $6.356 billion, equating to $28 per share . The consideration structure comprises 30 percent cash (US$21.2 million net, with US$10 million payable within ten days of conditions precedent and US$11.2 million in three equal instalments over 18 months) and 70 percent equity swap through the issuance of 46.8 million new CBZHL ordinary shares to NSSA on the basis of 28 CBZHL shares for every 95 FMHL shares, representing 8.23 percent dilution to existing CBZ shareholders . Prior to the transaction, CBZHL held 3.23 percent shareholding in FMHL through special purpose vehicle PIM Nominees (Private) Limited, bringing total post-acquisition shareholding to approximately 34.45 percent . Chairman Marc Holtzman stated the transaction aims to create 'one big, strong insurance company' through merging CBZ's insurance cluster (CBZ Life and CBZ Insurance) with FMHL—Zimbabwe's second-largest insurer commanding approximately 35 percent insurance industry market share with regional operations in Botswana, Malawi, Mozambique and Zambia—while CEO Blessing Mudavanhu noted the acquisition provides scale for insurance growth, genuine diversification for the financial holdings group, and regional presence previously absent, with a Johannesburg representative office established to lead regional integration with First Mutual subsidiaries . The transaction was adjudged fair and reasonable by independent financial advisors BDO Tax and Advisory Services . CBZHL intends to consolidate its position in FMHL by acquiring a control block of 35 percent as defined under the Companies and Other Business Entities Act, which will trigger a mandatory offer to remaining minority shareholders and culminate in FMHL's delisting from the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange . Following the transfer of shares, CBZ Holdings Limited became a significant shareholder of First Mutual Holdings Limited, with all required approvals obtained and administrative aspects concluded by September 2023 . The Competition and Tariff Commission subsequently commenced investigation into CBZ's proposed acquisition of additional shareholding in FMHL in June 2024, pursuant to conditions set in the 2022 merger approval requiring CBZ to notify the commission of any intention to increase its stake

headline: Ex-Econet employee faces fraud charges
content: A former Econet Wireless Zimbabwe employee, Christopher Nyamugafata of Glen View 2, Harare, appeared at the Harare Magistrates Court charged with fraud before magistrate Mrs Yeukai Dzuda, who remanded him to February 18, 2022. The State, led by Mr Thomas Chanakira, alleges that in November 2019 Nyamugafata was employed by Econet as an independent contractor in the customer care department, with duties including receiving call and internet queries from Econet customers on Ecocash transaction queries and forwarding them to the relevant office for Ecocash reversals. When Ecocash queries increased, Nyamugafata was entrusted with one supervisor's access credentials to facilitate Ecocash reversals. Sometime in November 2021, Nyamugafata allegedly decided to fraudulently use his supervisor's credentials to defraud Econet, purchasing 12 SIM cards which he used for Ecocash airtime purchase reversals, approaching different Econet line users advertising and selling airtime using his 12 Ecocash accounts, and reversing the transactions after purchase. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: Cafca optimistic about growth in 2022
content: Cafca Limited projects demand growth of 3.85 percent to 2,700 tonnes in 2022 from 2,600 tonnes in the prior comparable period, with company secretary Caroline Kangara stating the company is well positioned to meet anticipated demand with three months of finished goods stock cover serving as a hedging strategy against inflation, and has secured loans and overdraft facilities amounting to $320 million to improve financial position for operational execution. Kangara acknowledged mounting pressure to consolidate market share in the region as competitor numbers and volumes continue to grow, stating: 'The company's management believes that despite Covid 19 the business will remain in operation for the foreseeable future. The company will continue operating as a going concern as demand for cable has remained firm with sales volumes projected to increase... We have budgeted for a modest increase in volumes in 2021/22 against a background of uncertainty in the foreign currency availability and pricing as well as the impact of increased competition in our regional markets.' Despite challenges, the company will take advantage of prospects in local and export markets (Southern and Central Africa, EU and Russia) through adequate mitigating strategies against the challenging local economic environment. In 2021, 95.8 percent of produce was consumed locally with the balance exported. The company manufactures over 900 cabling products to British and South African markets including 11KV XLPE cables, and recovers decommissioned cables for recycling. This positive outlook follows CAFCA's 45 percent export volume increase in 2020 (CAFCA001) and its strategic positioning as a key supplier to ZETDC (CAFCA004).

headline: Omicron deadlier, no room for complacency: Mahomva
content: Omicron deadlier, no room for complacency: Mahomva","National Covid-19 coordinator Dr Agnes Mahomva warned that the fourth wave triggered by the Omicron variant has proven deadlier than the Beta variant which fuelled the second wave during the festive season in December 2020, with a 14-day analysis from December 20, 2021 to January 2, 2022 showing 245 Covid-19 deaths recorded compared to 73 during the same period in the second wave, and daily average cases at 1,580 (total 22,121) compared to a 167 daily average resulting in 2,346 cases over the same period previously. Health experts stated that almost 90 percent of Covid-19 fatalities are among people who were not vaccinated, with research showing unvaccinated patients make up more than 90 percent of hospital admissions and 88.9 percent of deaths. Dr Mahomva emphasized that vaccination remains the best protection, stating 'we cannot relax or be complacent... it is not over until it is over.' Community Working Group on Health director Mr Itai Rusike urged communities not to lower their guard as Omicron causes illness, hospitalisation and death just like any other variant, and to leave medical diagnosis to experts while observing recommended preventive measures. Bulawayo Provincial Medical Director Dr Maphios Siamuchembu stated any variant of Covid-19 is deadly and there is no predicting how many people will be killed, discouraging congregating which fuels transmission rates. Zimbabwe recorded 5,032 deaths to date, with the highest single-day deaths (102) recorded on June 16, 2021. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: Afdis to leverage on generated forex, new plant
content: New plant; US$1m cider plant commissioned, wines +67%","African Distillers Limited will continue to leverage on foreign currency generated from trading to cushion against imported inputs shortage and supply chain costs, having commissioned its US$1 million cider fermentation plant for Hunters Dry and Hunters Gold brands using inputs sourced locally—a strategic milestone first announced in January 2022 (AFDIS010) and confirmed for Q1 2022 commissioning. The company reported in a trading update for the quarter ended December 31, 2021 that revenue increased 57 percent for the quarter and 52 percent for the nine months in inflation-adjusted terms (148 percent and 159 percent in historic terms) driven by volume growth from firm demand over the festive season. Wine volume grew 67 percent mainly driven by 4th Street due to improved availability and affordability following the local production project commissioned during the quarter—executing the November 2021 localisation strategy (AFDIS014)—while spirits and ready-to-drink volumes grew 17 percent and 41 percent respectively, curtailed by supply constraints from regional glass pack shortages. Total volume growth registered 32 percent for the quarter and 48 percent for the nine months compared to prior year, with the trading environment stable and relaxation of COVID-19 lockdown restrictions increasing economic activity, further improved by increased activity in key sectors including agriculture, mining and infrastructure projects. The country experienced a fourth wave of COVID-19 towards the end of the quarter causing business disruptions through higher employee absenteeism, with the full impact of the pandemic remaining uncertain as the company continues implementing mitigatory measures for business continuity. Management stated it will continue focusing on strategies that grow market share and enhance shareholder value despite high inflation, depreciating exchange rate and COVID-19 effects, benefiting from growth in key sectors of the economy.

headline: CAFCA stocks up to hedge against inflation
content: CAFCA Limited chief executive Robert Webster told shareholders at the Annual General Meeting that the company's finished goods inventory stands at 671 tonnes, serving as a hedge against inflation and ensuring customer requirements are met, providing 2.5 months cover against current monthly sales of approximately 225 tonnes. Webster noted that the company has capacity to sell 1,309 tonnes per month. Profit before tax for the period to January 2022 surged 161 percent to $485 million compared to the prior year period. January volumes reached 172 tonnes against 164 tonnes in the previous year, while February volumes hit 235 tonnes against a mere 2.6 tonnes in the prior year period. Webster forecast six monthly volume increase of approximately 10 percent over the previous year's six monthly volume, with the next three months sales volume expected to exceed current quarterly sales. For the financial year ended September 30, 2021, volumes increased 49 percent from 1,744 tonnes to 2,604 tonnes driven by improvement in economic activity coming out of pandemic lockdown. Shareholders endorsed $6.2 million paid as directors fees for the past year and $7.2 million remuneration to PwC for the past audit. They also approved the resignation of PwC as auditors after long service, appointing Grant Thornton (Zimbabwe) as auditors for the ensuing year. This follows CAFCA's 45 percent export volume increase in 2020 (CAFCA001) and its strategic positioning as key supplier to ZETDC (CAFCA004)

headline: Delta to empower contracted farmers
content: Delta Corporation is working with banking partners to accelerate procurement of modern irrigation equipment for contracted commercial farmers to drive production of raw materials, having set aside US$3.9 million for the 2021/22 farming season under out-grower schemes targeting growers across the country . In its sustainability development report, Delta confirmed a farmer-centric approach to ensure 100 percent of direct farmers are skilled, connected and financially empowered, engaging over 9,000 farmers including commercial scale and communal smallholder farmers in barley, maize and sorghum contracting schemes . The 2021 summer contracts for maize and sorghum are set to achieve output and yield targets, benefiting from good rains and stable electricity supply, following the 2020 winter barley and summer cropping programmes which were adversely affected by drought leading to lower produce quality and yields, though the country remains self-sufficient on barley covering both domestic and export malt requirements . Delta requires approximately 11,400 tonnes of red sorghum and 2,400 tonnes of white sorghum annually, with the sorghum contract scheme aiming to integrate marginalised communities through value chains and promote small grain cultivation . Recent data confirms Delta contracted 7,000 hectares under barley producing 35,000 tonnes against annual requirements of 22,000 tonnes, 2,000 hectares under maize for 14,000 tonnes (US$2.749 million allocation), and 8,835 hectares under sorghum for 14,421 tonnes (US$1.163 million allocation), with benefits including security of supply, desired quality at best cost, increased farmer returns, enhanced food security and import substitution saving foreign currency

headline: Employee defrauds Econet of $4 million
Athletes ready for CBZ Marathon
content: CBZ Holdings' third edition of the CBZ Marathon is set for March 20, 2022 at Old Georgians Sports Club in Harare, attracting 1,000 athletes after the event could not take place in 2021 due to COVID-19 lockdown restrictions, according to CBZ group executive marketing and corporate affairs Matilda Nyathi. The event, organised by Harare Athletics Club, features the main 42.2km marathon, half-marathon, 10km fun run and 5km fun run, including handicapped wheelchair runners. Winners' prizes will be awarded to the top three overall women and top three overall men across all categories including 5km, 10km, half-marathon, wheelchair and marathon, with the highest prize reserved for the marathon followed by the half-marathon and subsequent races. Correctional Stars Athletics Club coach Collen Makaza confirmed that several athletes including Jonathan Chinyoka (2020 marathon winner), Caroline Mhandu, Kelvin Pangiso and Nomore Wiriki are competing, with Chinyoka targeting the half-marathon as preparation for the Two Oceans Ultra-marathon on April 17, 2022 in Cape Town and aiming for a time of 1 hour 4 minutes. Nyathi confirmed registration has closed after reaching the targeted 1,000 participants. This event follows CBZ's inaugural 2019 marathon which attracted 1,400 participants (CBZ038, CBZ047), the 2020 marathon which attracted 1,750 athletes and served as the launch platform for CBZ's new brand with CEO Blessing Mudavanhu confirmation (CBZ027, CBZ034), and demonstrates sustained brand investment and community engagement despite pandemic disruptions.

headline: About OK Zimbabwe
content: OK Zimbabwe Limited is Zimbabwe's leading supermarket retailer, trading under three brands: OK Stores, Bon Marché Stores, and OK Mart. Founded in 1942 with its inaugural branch in Harare (then Salisbury), the company has operated for over 80 years. It was first incorporated as Springmaster Corporation in 1953, renamed Deltrade Limited in 1984, and adopted its current name in July 2001. Delta Corporation acquired the business in 1977 and held it until the de-merger in October 2001. The group operates across three major categories: groceries (dry groceries, butchery, delicatessen, bakery, fruit/vegetables), basic clothing and textiles, and houseware products. CEO Max Karombo (appointed April 2021) brings extensive experience from Unilever (Marketing Director, Customer Development Director, MD Unilever Uganda, MD Unilever Zimbabwe) and Delta Corporation (10 years as Executive Director – Operations and Marketing). The group has developed own brands through Bon Marché's Premier Choice and Shopperschoice labels. As of 2023, OK serves approximately 4 million customers monthly across 52 stores, with close to 90% of products locally sourced .

headline: Econet Insurance launches CitySure
content: Econet Insurance, a subsidiary of EcoCash Holdings Zimbabwe, has partnered with Capitol Insurance Brokers and City of Harare to launch CitySure, a one-stop-platform enabling residents to purchase insurance cover and pay monthly council bills under one roof, leveraging technology and innovation to drive mass access and insurance inclusion for underserved communities, according to EcoCash Holdings chief executive Mr Eddie Chibi. In the first phase, residents can buy short-term insurance packages including vehicle, home insurance and cover for personal belongings, with subsequent phases unveiling life and health insurance packages and diaspora remittance cash collection. Capitol Insurance Brokers managing director Mr Philip Mutasa stated the partnership targets untapped high-density markets to demystify insurance and increase penetration, while also catering for high-end markets with gated communities and rapid response security programmes, delivering insurance at the doorstep with digital efficiency in premium payment and claims settlement. Insurance and Pensions Commission Director for Insurance and Microinsurance Mrs Sibongile Siwela commended the initiative, noting that a 2019 baseline survey found distribution channels were key barriers to insurance uptake and CitySure will help break that barrier, facilitating access for Harare residents in their homes with intention to scale to other towns and cities. Secretary for Provincial Affairs and Devolution for Harare Metropolitan Mr Tafadzwa Muguti confirmed the tripartite partnership will create a win-win situation with council realising 10 percent commission on premiums ring-fenced for insurance and risk management, plus refurbishment of council facilities and employment creation for young people in residential districts. EcoCash Holdings chief commercial officer Mr Gilbert Tsongorera indicated CitySure will be launched in other cities, towns and rural areas countrywide, with group products ranging from mobile money platforms, agriculture to education to be available at council offices. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: BAT volumes in 25pc growth
content: BAT Zimbabwe chairman Mr Lovemore Manatsa reported in the group's 2021 annual report that the company recorded 25 percent volume growth compared to the prior year, driven by firm consumer demand for its products, increased export of cut-rag tobacco (up 74 percent), consistent product supply, and the easing of COVID-19 lockdown restrictions which brought a decline in inflation rate, stability of the exchange rate, and increased economic and social activity. Revenue increased 42 percent to $1.4 billion driven by volume growth, price reviews and revenue generated from export of cut-rag tobacco and leaf, resulting in gross profit increase of $1.3 billion compared to 2020. Selling and marketing costs increased 39 percent ($172 million) driven by trade activations during the launch of brand migration from Everest Menthol to Lucky Strike, other marketing investments and strategic initiatives responding to consumer preferences.
