Administrative expenses remained flat and other losses decreased by $539 million due to stabilisation of the local currency, resulting in operating profit increasing by $1.8 billion from $412 million in the prior year. Subsequent to December 31, 2019, the RBZ registered blocked funds amounting to US$15.7 million in respect of outstanding dividends, in line with Exchange Control Directive RU28 (February 21, 2019) and Exchange Control Circular 8 (July 24, 2019), with RBZ finalising appropriate instruments to facilitate settlement. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, Seed Co, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: Profiling ZSE Top 10 Index Constituents: British American Tobacco Zimbabwe
Chibuku Neshamwari dance festival is back!
content: Delta Beverages has announced the return of the Chibuku Neshamwari Traditional Dance Festival after a three-year hiatus caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, with the first round of provincial competitions scheduled for May 28, 2022 across the country's 10 provinces, culminating in national finals where provincial winners will battle for prize monies. The competition, launched in 1963 and now in its 59th year, operates through a tripartite partnership between Delta Beverages, the National Arts Council of Zimbabwe and the Zimbabwe National Traditional Dance Association, serving as a launchpad for traditional dance groups to gain global recognition and performances at corporate and cultural events. Bulawayo-based dance group Ezimnyama are the reigning champions, having won the national competition in 2019—their second victory after emerging victorious in 2017. Delta Beverages stated that the festival has become important in promoting and preserving traditional dances as crucial intangible cultural heritage, keeping alive the many traditional dances that define the country and its people through the way people interact, celebrate and narrate their past. This sponsorship revival follows Delta's December 2019 confirmation of the Castle Lager Premier Soccer League three-year deal renewal through 2022 (DLTA013), the November 2022 Soccer Stars of the Year awards return (DLTA019), and the company's extensive 125-year operating history and corporate social responsibility portfolio including the Melfort Old People's Home (DLTA012) and scholarship schemes (DLTA015)

headline: Irrigation scheme cultivates harmony among families
content: SeedCo contracts Maunganidze","Maunganidze Irrigation Scheme, a 65.5-hectare smallholder enterprise established in 1997 with European Union funding benefiting 84 farmers (32 female, 52 male) on 0.8-hectare plots per household in Chief Mutema's area, Chipinge District, was severely damaged by Cyclone Idai in 2019 with destroyed canals, boreholes, perimeter fencing and flooded fields, reducing operating capacity below 50 percent and causing food insecurity, increased gender-based violence, school dropouts particularly affecting girls, and loss of livelihoods. The World Bank-funded Zimbabwe Idai Recovery Project, implemented through WFP, FAO and UNOPS, rehabilitated drainage storms, drilled boreholes equipped with submersible pumps, installed 237 solar panels reducing electricity bills by 85 percent, repaired damaged canals and fencing, increasing irrigable area by 50 percent. All 84 farmers received input packs (fertilisers, maize and sugar bean seeds), training in agronomy, good agricultural practices, safe pesticide use, agribusiness development and market linkages. Farmers now produce on commercial scale with SeedCo contracting fine beans (5 hectares, 1 tonne/hectare yield) and sugar beans (15 hectares under SeedCo contract, 29 hectares total, 1.5 tonnes/hectare yield) , alongside 35 hectares tomatoes (30 tonnes/hectare) for informal markets and Mozambique exports, and 1.5 hectares onions (40 tonnes/hectare). In 2021, farmers mobilised US$7,000 to drill borehole number 13 independently. Between 2020-2021, the scheme hosted representatives from 42 national irrigation schemes on governance and sustainability learning visits. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned, though Seed Co International (SEED) is referenced as a commercial contracting partner

headline: Econet Vic Falls Marathon set to attract 5 000 participants
content: Econet Wireless Zimbabwe's 2022 Victoria Falls Marathon, scheduled for July 3, 2022, is expected to draw over 5,000 registered participants from around the world in the first in-person event since 2019 following the COVID-19 enforced break in 2020 and 2021, with many athletes bringing friends and family meaning the actual visitor numbers to the resort town could be well over 5,000. Econet chief operating officer Mr Kezito Makuni, an ardent marathon runner himself, stated this year's marathon is special as it marks the reopening of borders and economies and the return of visitors to Zimbabwe's tourist destinations following the pandemic, along with the economic benefits it brings to Vic Falls and the hospitality and tourism sector. Now widely regarded as Africa's premier marathon due to its unique setting in the iconic Victoria Falls and growing numbers of local and international runners, the event is entirely supported by local organisations, creating business and local employment opportunities for the Vic Falls community. The marathon has earned recognition from World Athletics and the Association of International Marathons and Distance Races, featuring a rebranded 'Elephant route' (42km full marathon), 'Leopard route' (21km half marathon), 'Lion route' (7.5km Family Fun Run), and a half marathon 10.5km x 2 relay. Econet has sponsored the marathon since 2009, using it as a platform to encourage healthy lifestyles and active exercise. The last event before the pandemic in 2019 attracted over 3,500 participants from more than 40 countries. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned, though CBZ has sponsored its own Harare-based marathon since 2019 (CBZ027, CBZ034, CBZ038, CBZ047).

headline: Delta unit sees 16pc decline in volumes
content: National Breweries, a Zambian unit of Delta Corporation, recorded a 16 percent volume decline for the year ended March 31, 2022, leading to an 18 percent revenue contraction from US$16.76 million in 2020 to US$13.55 million in 2021. The company attributed the performance to the decline in informal exports of Chibuku Super to Zimbabwe, the increase in excise and general product prices, and COVID-19 restrictions which affected product availability in the market, compounded by resurgence of competition from other bulk beer offerings that are not excise/VAT compliant. Natbrew began exporting Chibuku Super to Zimbabwe in the fourth quarter of the fiscal year, a strategy expected to contribute to volume growth while earning valuable foreign currency to fund working capital requirements. Margins were under pressure from increased raw material prices in the second half of the year and distribution costs which peaked in the last quarter due to fuel price increases. The appreciation of the Kwacha post-elections resulted in a significant exchange gain of US$2.6 million on revaluation of foreign currency liabilities. The company ended the year with an operational loss of US$5 million, compared to US$9.8 million the previous year, with intercompany borrowings resulting in a 70 percent increase in net financing expenses, and a loss after tax of US$7 million and loss per share of US$0.11, compared to US$11.29 million and US$0.11 the previous year. The company's outlook indicates intensified efforts to drive volume growth through quality and competitively priced products, improving product availability by focusing on outlet penetration, with full benefits of strategies expected in the next financial year and beyond when financial performance and cash flows are expected to improve. This operational update follows Delta's extensive regional footprint including Natbrew Zambia and United National Breweries South Africa, and the company's broader agricultural empowerment initiatives (DLTA028).

headline: OK to continue expansion despise inflation challenges
content: "OK Zimbabwe CEO Maxen Karombo confirmed the group will continue expansion plans despite high inflation and exchange rate volatility, with refurbishments and new stores scheduled for FY2023. New stores opened: Banket (Harare-Chirundu Highway) and OK Mart in Chivhu. Technology investment planned to improve operational efficiencies. FY2022 results showed resilience: sales volume +22.7%, revenue +34.7% to $79.9bn (inflation-adjusted), PBT +38.5% to $4.8bn, PAT +48.9% to $2.8bn. Overheads +37% driven by staff costs, electricity, rentals, and IMTT (transaction tax). Chairman Herbert Nkala described 34.3% effective tax rate as 'unsustainable' and reiterated calls for IMTT relief. Capex reached $3.1bn (+48% y/y) for refurbishments and new stores. FBC Securities flagged risks: informal sector competition, shrinking disposable incomes, and reduced agricultural yields. Karombo emphasized cost containment and stakeholder alignment to deliver sustainable growth.

headline: CBZ declares $1 billion annual dividend
content: CBZ Holdings declared a $1 billion financial dividend for the year ended December 31, 2021, representing $191.57 cents per share, payable on or about June 30, 2022 to all shareholders registered as at close of business on June 24, 2022, with shares trading cum-dividend on the ZSE up to June 21, 2022 and ex-dividend from June 22, 2022, according to group corporate legal secretary Ms Rumbidzayi Jakanani. The group's total income increased to $42.5 billion from $23.5 billion in 2020, driven by significant growth in interest income which rose to $21.8 billion from $8.1 billion, underpinned by growth in interest from loans to $14.8 billion from $2.9 billion in 2020. Non-interest income at $23 billion was the largest contributor to total income, with commission and fee income of $9.2 billion leading, followed by agro-business income of $4.5 billion—directly attributable to CBZ Agro-Yield's Command Agriculture/NEAPS financing operations—and unrealised profit on foreign currency exchange of $4.1 billion. Chairman Mr Marc Holtzman stated the group will continue with the acquisition transaction of First Mutual Holdings (FML), having entered into a share purchase agreement in October 2021 to acquire NSSA's 31.22 percent stake, which will create a critical mass factor allowing the group to achieve even more positive results. This dividend declaration demonstrates CBZ's sustained profitability and shareholder returns despite the challenging operating environment documented throughout your corpus, following the group's US$14 million to US$500 million market capitalisation re-rating under Holtzman's chairmanship (CBZ010) and preceding the December 2023 strategic leadership transition where Holtzman retired and Dr Blessing Mudavanhu stepped down as Group CEO (CBZ079).

headline: Tarakinyu ready for Econet Victoria Falls Marathon
content: The Econet Victoria Falls Marathon returned as a physical event for the first time since 2019, following a two-year COVID-19 hiatus, with 5,000 participants expected on July 3, 2022 [citation:article]. Events include 42.195km marathon, half marathon, fun run, and half marathon relay. Athlete Moses Tarakinyu (Black Rhinos AC), winner of the 2019 half marathon, aims for a personal best time [citation:article]. The marathon, sponsored by Econet since 2009, attracts international participants and delivers significant economic impact to Victoria Falls. For Econet, the sponsorship builds brand equity, aligns with wellness positioning, and demonstrates commitment to national tourism and sport. The event's return signals post-COVID normalization and Econet's continued support for community events. Subsequent 2023 edition attracted over 4,500 runners from 42 countries, with COO Kezito Makuni linking it to the national US$5bn tourism vision [citation:ECO164]."

headline: Econet Victoria Falls Marathon lives up to its billing
content: The 2022 Econet Victoria Falls Marathon attracted over 4,500 local and foreign athletes from more than 30 countries, making a strong return after a three-year absence due to COVID-19 pandemic. Moses Tarakinyu defended his 21km race title with a time of 01:03:30, followed by Isaac Mpofu and Wellington Varevi. In the female 21km category, Caroline Mhandu clocked 01:18:50, with Bertha Sitariko and Constance Nyasango completing the podium. Jonathan Chinyoka won the full marathon 42km in 02:18:10 ahead of Blessing Waison and Kelvin Pangiso, while Patience Murove claimed the female 42km title with a personal best 02:47:31 outrunning Olivia Chitate and Ethel Sibanda. Econet Chief Operating Officer Kezito Makuni stated the event's success demonstrated the marathon's 'pulling power, and its position as one of Africa's premier racing events,' noting that nearly 5,000 athletes were targeted and some late registrants were turned away as registration had closed. Makuni reiterated Econet will continue to support the marathon to promote wellness and market Zimbabwe as a prime tourist destination, giving a much-needed fillip to the tourism industry which official figures show lost more than US$1 billion between 2020 and 2021 due to pandemic travel restrictions. Victoria Falls Mayor Councillor Somveli Dlamini commended Econet and organizers for bringing tourists back to the resort town, stating 'It is commendable that we have risen above our challenges and have been given another shot of life after two depressing years. The Econet Victoria Falls Marathon continues to draw huge numbers of spectators and participants across the world, a crucial activity for the promotion of tourism.' No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned.

headline: Dairibord CEO retires
content: Anthony Mandiwanza, Dairibord's CEO of 24 years (34 years total service), retired effective September 2022. He presided over the company's privatisation, ZSE listing (1997), and diversification from a predominantly milk business into broader agro-industry areas [citation:article]. He also chaired Barclays Bank Zimbabwe and TSL boards [citation:article]. Incoming CEO Mercy Ndoro, previously Finance Director, brings 25+ years of Dairibord experience (Financial Accountant, Finance Executive, Company Secretary). She holds a B.Accountancy Honours and MBA from University of Zimbabwe [citation:article]. Godfrey Machazi appointed Chief Operating Officer effective June 1, 2022 [citation:article]. The transition ensures leadership continuity with deep institutional knowledge. Ndoro's subsequent performance (FY2023 revenue +47%, PAT +268% by 2024) validated the appointment [citation:DZL170].

headline: Suspended Harare town clerk Chisango acquitted
content: Suspended Harare City Council town clerk Hosiah Chisango, suspended director of housing and community services Addmore Nhekairo and town planner Samuel Nyabezi have been acquitted by Harare magistrate Mr Taurai Manuwere in a case they were accused of issuing stands in Kuwadzana to land developing company Bluffhope Investments without following proper procedures, with the magistrate ruling that the trio did not offend the law and acted within the provisions of their duties as council officials regarding availing of residential stands in Kuwadzana 3. The State had alleged that sometime in 2019, the trio effected a change of use plan for land without seeking Government approval as required under the Urban Councils Act, that Chisango handed over 13 provisional allocation letters to Bluffhope Investments managing director Ms Getrude Dumba and later instructed her to deposit $130,000 into City of Harare's bank account for intrinsic value of the land and another $100,000 into a corporate bank account belonging to GlenRoy, and that Nhekairo allegedly instructed Ms Dumba to deposit $100,000 into a CBZ bank account registered in the name of Kennedy Teerechire, with the money deposited into both accounts on December 5, 2019. The defence, with Chisango represented by Advocate Ray Goba, Nhekairo by Mr Batanai Pesanai and Nyabezi by Mr Simon Chabuka, successfully argued that Chisango acted according to his duties and responsibilities as enshrined in the Urban Councils Act, that there was no documentary evidence linking Nhekairo to the alleged offences or basis to suggest he received a bribe, and that the State failed to prove criminal conduct. CBZ Bank is named solely as the financial institution holding the account into which one of the allegedly improper payments was deposited, with no allegation of wrongdoing against the bank presented, and the court's acquittal removes any taint of impropriety regarding the transaction.

headline: OK Bazaars achieves 95pc local product supplies
content: A retrospective article from The Herald (July 12, 1979) revealed that OK Bazaars Ltd achieved over 95% local product supplies in the year to March 1979, with local purchases totaling $14.1 million. Men's shirts local content increased from 60% to 80% in one year; women's clothing ranged from 60-100% locally manufactured. The report noted 'manufacturers, in conjunction with buyers, are producing first class garments and the amount of locally produced cotton is increasing at a very rapid rate.' The 'Lessons for Today' section draws parallels to modern OK Zimbabwe, which continues to source approximately 90% of products locally . The commentary emphasizes benefits: foreign currency savings, job creation, economic growth, and bridging producer-consumer gaps. It calls for government support for local procurement and contract farming partnerships. This historical context demonstrates OK Zimbabwe's long-standing commitment to local sourcing, a strategic advantage that reduces forex exposure and aligns with national development goals."

headline: Afdis leans on forex earnings
content: Afdis leans on forex earnings; volumes +18%, wines +30% on 4th Street localisation","African Distillers Limited will continue to leverage foreign currency generated from trading to ensure continuous supply of imported inputs, with company secretary Ms Lydiah Mutamuko reporting in a trading update for the quarter ended June 30, 2022 that volumes grew 18 percent compared to the same period prior year, benefiting from improved supply into the market and increased outdoor activities as COVID-19 restrictions were relaxed. Wines volume grew 30 percent over prior year, driven by 4th Street wine due to improved availability and affordability following the commissioning of the brand's local production—executing the November 2021 localisation strategy (AFDIS014) targeting 4th Street previously imported from South Africa—while spirits and ready-to-drink volumes grew 23 percent and 12 percent respectively on strong demand and increased market penetration. Revenue grew 57 percent in inflation-adjusted terms and 285 percent in historic terms due to improved demand and inflation-related adjustments, with Mutamuko noting the operating environment remains challenging with inflation and power shortages impacting business, though the legislated multi-currency regime will aid business planning. Management continues implementing initiatives focusing on revenue and profitability growth through product innovation, market share protection and cost containment to enhance shareholder value, with the company having commissioned its US$1 million cider fermentation plant for Hunters Dry and Hunters Gold brands using inputs sourced locally—a project first announced in January 2022 (AFDIS010) and confirmed for Q3 2022 commissioning at the July 2022 AGM (AFDIS015).

headline: All set for Chibuku Neshamwari
content: Delta Corporation announced preparations for the Chibuku Neshamwari National Final, scheduled for August 13, 2022, at Harare Gardens, marking the 60th anniversary of the Chibuku brand. Ten provincial finalists will compete for the top prize of US$15,000 [citation:3]. The event features the Chibuku Big 60 Promotion Grand Draw, where four consumers will win brand new Nissan Navara single cabs [citation:2][citation:5]. Patricia Murambinda, Delta General Manager Corporate Affairs, confirmed seven dance genres will be showcased. The festival, launched in 1963 and sponsored by Delta for nearly 25 years [citation:3], returned after a two-year COVID-19 hiatus [citation:6][citation:10]. Subsequently, Dapurahunanzva Dance Group from Manicaland won the national finals, with three women among the four vehicle winners [citation:2][citation:3]. Delta's Chibuku brand commemorated six decades of traditional brewing, evolving from its original form to include flavoured variants and PET packaging [citation:3].

headline: Resumption of social activities after restrictions benefit Delta
content: Afdis volumes +18%","Delta Corporation reported strong first quarter performance for the period ended June 30, 2022 as the relaxation of COVID-19 restrictions drove increased social activities and movement of people, with sorghum beer volume in Zimbabwe growing 14 percent driven by the standard Chibuku (Scud) product and the Chibuku brand's 60th anniversary celebrations, while Chibuku Super remains constrained by limited production capacity with a new plant being installed at Harare brewery for commissioning in early 2023 and the Chibuku Super Banana flavour launched in June 2022 already 'exciting the market.' Lager beer volume grew 19 percent on improved supply of brands and packs benefiting from injection of returnable glass, with intermittent supply gaps arising from limited packaging capacity ahead of new plant installation in early 2023 and the recently launched Sable lager bringing excitement for its 'smooth taste and easy drinking credentials,' while sparkling beverages volume grew 32 percent recovering market share through consistent product supply and expanded pack and flavour offering. Associate company African Distillers Limited recorded 18 percent volume growth for the quarter, with cider supply having stabilised after the previous quarter's glass bottle outage and the business continuing to benefit from local production of some key brands—executing the 4th Street localisation strategy (AFDIS014, AFDIS015, AFDIS016, AFDIS017) and US$1 million cider fermentation plant commissioning (AFDIS010, AFDIS017). Associate Schweppes volume grew 9 percent constrained by fruit juice shortages for flagship Mazoe Orange Crush, with local supply resumed as harvesting commenced in June 2022. United National Breweries South Africa recorded 13 percent volume growth despite adverse weather, while Natbrew Zambia volumes declined 9 percent following January 2022 price increases responding to excise duty hikes, with signs of recovery through broadening product offering, revamped route to market and regional Chibuku Super exports. Group revenue grew 55 percent in inflation-adjusted terms (283 percent historical cost) driven by volume growth and replacement cost-based pricing, with consumer spending remaining high reflecting faster velocity of the local currency spurred by increased mining activity, infrastructure projects, marketing of commercial crops and foreign currency wage payments, though demand constrained by declining disposable incomes in certain consumer groups as inflation erodes earnings. Delta remains upbeat but notes disparities in exchange rate and lack of clarity in currency payment legislation for certain taxes creates significant uncertainties and business risks, while poised to exploit opportunities from infrastructure development projects, mining activities and diaspora remittances.

headline: Afdis positioned for further growth
content: Afdis positioned for further growth; revenue surges fivefold, 4th Street localisation gains traction","African Distillers Limited reported a fivefold increase in historical revenue to $1.9 billion in July 2022 from prior year comparative, with managing director Stanley Muchenje informing shareholders at the company's annual general meeting that the business is well positioned to continue exploiting opportunities for further growth through market penetration, direct service distribution to peri-urban and rural markets, and growing existing customer relationships. Muchenje acknowledged the challenging operating environment including the temporary suspension of lending facilities announced in May 2022—initially a ban on bank lending to stem local currency decline which was later upheld after an outcry—which constrained working capital access and forced suppliers to demand prepayment, though Afdis continued operating and generating foreign currency from trade to access important raw materials, noting there is 'underlying resilience in the economy' allowing growth opportunities. The 4th Street brand, localised from South African imports as announced in November 2021 (AFDIS014), is gaining traction and much more available across the market, while the company's US$1 million cider fermentation plant for Hunters Dry and Hunters Gold brands—first announced in January 2022 (AFDIS010) and nearing completion—is scheduled for commissioning in Q3 2022, using inputs sourced locally to produce affordable ranges cheaper and access more customers. Company secretary Lydiah Mutamuko reported in a Q2 2022 trading update that volumes grew 18 percent compared to the same period prior year, benefiting from improved supply into the market and increased outdoor activities as COVID-19 restrictions relaxed, with management continuing to implement initiatives focusing on revenue and profitability growth through product innovation, market share protection and cost containment to enhance shareholder value. The AGM endorsed $14.3 million paid as directors' fees for the past year's audit and $23.7 million remuneration paid to Ernst & Young Chartered Accountants.

headline: Counting the blue chips on ZSE
content: Investment research firm Morgan&Co Research has identified blue chip stocks on the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange using criteria of large market capitalisation, high liquidity, little or no debt, stable debt-to-equity ratio, high return on equity, and stable dividend pay-outs. Based on share prices as at July 29, 2022, the top 15 stocks by market capitalisation include Econet, Delta, Innscor, Ecocash, Simbisa Brands, National Foods, Padenga, CBZ Holdings, BAT Zimbabwe, Hippo, SeedCo International, FBCH, Axia, African Distillers Limited, and CFI. However, when applying the liquidity criterion—measured by each stock's contribution to daily traded value over six months—stocks contributing less than 5 percent are disqualified, removing CBZ Holdings and African Distillers Limited from blue chip consideration, while Econet, Delta, Innscor, Simbisa Brands, Ecocash, and Hippo progress to fundamental analysis. Delta emerges as a clear blue chip with debt-to-equity ratio averaging 16 percent (2010-2016), consistent 25 percent return on equity, and consistent dividends forfeited only once in 2020 due to COVID-19. Innscor meets strict definition with stable leverage and return on equity, last forfeiting a dividend in 2009. Econet's fundamentals are commendable with operating profit margin averaging 22 percent (2014-2019), though foreign currency-denominated debentures have muddled leverage and affected dividend pay-outs, expected to resume at 17 percent pay-out ratio post-2022. Ecocash and Simbisa are considered too soon to prove as standalone businesses, while Hippo is disqualified due to cascading uncertainty from parent company Tongaat Hulett's financial challenges and 2019 listing suspension.

headline: Seed Co taps into growth opportunities
content: Seed Co International Limited, a leading seed producer in Zimbabwe and Africa, is positioning to capitalise on growth opportunities created by supply disruptions from the Russia-Ukraine war, with board chairman David Long stating that Africa's agricultural sector is now receiving more attention to enhance production and reduce import dependence as nearly 50 countries depend on Russia and Ukraine for at least 30 percent of their wheat imports . The group's strategic positioning at the genesis of the food chain is expected to be supported by anticipated forced import substitution and increased investment in agriculture to plug the supply gap, though the extent of growth opportunity is expected to be diluted by unavoidable imported inflation offsetting higher commodity prices and increased investment . Despite the headwinds, revenue grew 36 percent in the first quarter compared to 2021 on price adjustments to preserve value, with operating profit increasing 3.3 times from a loss position in 2021, attributable to margin recovery . However, total sales volumes decreased 18 percent with wheat seed comprising just under 90 percent of volume, driven by a 13 percent reduction in wheat seed sales due to stringent farmer selection measures by input funders and no repeat early legume and sorghum sales . Seed Co believes the central bank's 200 percent policy rate is a hindrance to financing productive sectors, with the Zimbabwean socio-economic environment remaining challenging with hyperinflation, wide disparity between official and alternative market exchange rates, and high interest rates making productive financing unviable . The group has adequate stocks both in Zimbabwe and on the continent to contribute meaningfully to primary food production subject to favourable climatic and economic conditions, leveraging production bases in Southern Africa to supply seed to East and West African markets facing shortages . CEO Morgan Nzwere previously noted that global supply shocks and imported inflation are expected to impact the cost of doing business and compound climate change effects, but regional food security remains top priority and the group will step up operations to satisfy anticipated increased seed demand in regional markets

headline: OK Zim well-funded for capex projects
content: OK Zimbabwe CEO Maxen Karombo confirmed at the AGM that the group has measures in place to cover working capital and planned capex, despite 'market's wide liquidity challenges.' Q1 2023 (ended June 30, 2022) showed volumes +1%, sales values +237% historical (+40% inflation-adjusted). Karombo addressed key challenges: 200% policy rate (from 80%) creating 'excessive borrowing costs' and liquidity pressure across supply chain; official exchange rate ($416/USD) less attractive than parallel market (~$900/USD), impacting USD price competitiveness and forex generation; and IMTT burden on formal sector. He noted representations made to Treasury for tax review. Margins remained consistent with prior year. Directors' fees approved at $48.5m, auditors' fees $30m. Outlook expects inflation headwinds to dominate short-term. The group's sound financial footing enables continued investment despite challenging environment.

headline: SeedCo Int sees strong demand for maize seed
content: Seed Co International Limited, listed on the Victoria Falls Stock Exchange, anticipates maize seed production volumes to increase by 6 percent to 34,000 metric tonnes in 2022 compared to the previous season, adequate to meet demand across its markets in Botswana, DRC, Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, Nigeria, Rwanda, South Africa, Tanzania, Zambia, and Zimbabwe. Group chief executive officer Morgan Nzwere told shareholders at the Annual General Meeting that most markets are currently off-season except Zambia where winter cereals are selling, with maize season set to commence in October when rains arrive in most markets. Regional business volume increased 11 percent year-to-date, entirely from Zambia. Nigeria maize seed sales were 13 percent lower due to stock shortages caused by a large government order in the prior year that wiped out carryover stocks, resulting in inadequate production to meet market demand. Kenya and Tanzania experienced second-season drought dampening sales. Mozambique, a new market, shows good signs of growth with improving margins as hybrid sales contribution increases. Research and development continues with potato trials underway, planned for commercialisation in three years. Seed intake from growers is progressing at approximately 35 percent for maize and 55 percent for soya beans. Working capital includes US$47 million in trade receivables with 50 percent collected. Total stocks, including carryover quantities, will reach approximately 52,000 MT—13 percent higher than prior year—adequate to meet anticipated demand and opportunistic sales. The Russia-Ukraine war creates opportunities for import substitution in grain, with many countries pursuing self-sufficiency, while increased business is expected in West Africa, Angola and DRC as COVID-19 funds stabilise and gravitate toward agriculture. For the full year to March 31, 2022, revenue was flat at US$88.5 million on reduced sales volume, helped by Zambian kwacha strengthening and business growth in Mozambique

headline: Bearish trends stalk ZSE stocks
content: Afdis remains flat at $300 amid broad market retreat","Bearish trends continued to haunt the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange for the week to September 21, 2022 with all benchmark indicators closing in negative territory following fiscal and monetary policy interventions by Government to address speculation and arbitrage that had driven inflation resurgence and Zimbabwe dollar depreciation, as the ZSE All Share Index retreated 7 percent to 10,143 points, ZSE Top 10 Index fell 6 percent to 5,615 points, ZSE Top 15 Index lost 7.6 percent to 6,360 points, Medium Cap declined 7.5 percent to 25,017 points, Small Cap dropped 8 percent to 448,267 points, and total market value retreated 6 percent to $1.2 trillion. African Distillers Limited remained flat at $300 alongside Art ($16.60) and Fidelity ($21), bucking the broad market decline that saw Simbisa fall heaviest at -30 percent to $96.62, Turnall -27 percent to $3.31, African Sun -24 percent to $11.40, Tanganda -23 percent to $80, GB Holdings -22 percent to $1.70, Axia -15 percent to $28.65, and Dairibord, FBC and FCB -7 percent each, while gains were recorded in Truworths (+15% to $1.95), Mashonaland Holdings (+8% to $5.50), EcoCash Holdings (+7% to $30), Delta (+3.6% to $139.21) and BFCA (+3.4% to $9) with Masimba retreating marginally despite a solid order book valued at US$145 million (74% increase from US$83 million in H1 2021) and FMP gaining 2 percent despite net property income decreasing 46 percent on 25 percent revenue growth. Fidelity's flat performance came despite net premium written decreasing 3 percent to $1.242 billion and total income growing 379 percent on positive investment returns from fair value gains and foreign exchange gains.

headline: CBZ concludes merger transaction
content: CBZ Holdings Limited has received both Government and board approval to merge the operations of CBZ Bank and CBZ Building Society into one entity, CBZ Bank Limited, following Finance and Economic Development Minister Professor Mthuli Ncube's approval on May 20, 2022, with the Building Society licence surrendered to the Registrar of Building Societies for cancellation in terms of Section 14(3) of the Building Societies Act (Chapter 24:02). Group legal corporate secretary Rumbidzayi Jakanani stated in a notice to shareholders that the amalgamation will be effected through consolidation of all assets, liabilities and equity of CBZ Bank Limited and CBZ Building Society into one entity, CBZ Bank Limited, completing a process that began in 2010 when the group started an operational integration process between the bank and the building society, resulting in the former offering a one-stop shop facility to its clients. The transaction aligns with chairman Marc Holtzman's dynamic strategic vision set out in 2020 to maintain and grow the financial institution's dominant market share of more than 23 percent, develop its IT framework, and integrate its businesses including the newly launched CBZ Capital, following Holtzman's observation that financial services are set to change more in the next 10 years than in the past 300 years. This merger follows CBZ's strategic trajectory of creating a domestic and regional multi-asset class business in the financial services sector, positioning CBZ Holdings as the premier financial services provider in Zimbabwe led by its flagship business CBZ Bank, aiming to reach global investors and become the conduit for foreign investment in Zimbabwe, as previously articulated in Holtzman's 2020 interviews (CBZ009, CBZ010, CBZ016) and preceding the December 2023 leadership transition (CBZ079)

headline: Econet celebrates top performing employees
content: Econet Wireless Zimbabwe hosted its half-yearly 'EcoStars Awards' ceremony in Harare on October 7, 2022, recognizing over 50 top individual performers and more than 10 teams for consistently exceeding performance expectations. Deputy CEO Roy Chimanikire emphasized the programme rewards talent and hard work, stating recipients are 'part of the Econet story.' COO Kezito Makuni highlighted recognition and work-life balance as key leadership priorities. Chief HR Officer Rosseweater Usayi noted the awards sustain a performance culture and acknowledged employee sacrifices and innovation benefiting customers and business. The company, a ZSE blue chip, previously won the 'Top Company of the Year Award' (2020, 2021). Broader HR initiatives include winning the 2022 LinkedIn 'Learning Champion Award' for Sub-Saharan Africa, joining UN Global Compact, receiving ESG awards, maintaining a 4.1/5 employee rating, and investing in solar base stations and e-waste recycling. Employee feedback on Glassdoor/Indeed shows mixed reviews on career growth but appreciation for work culture and learning opportunities.

headline: OK Zim cuts prices for Christmas cheer
content: OK Zimbabwe launched its 'Christmas Is Here!' promotional campaign (November 17 - December 31, 2022) across all 52 stores (OK, Bon Marché, OK Mart), offering 'rock bottom' pricing described as 'almost like giving away.' The campaign includes: instant till-point rewards, combos, hampers, free toys for children with purchases over $10,000, Santa Saturdays with surprise gifts, and free-access fan parks for the FIFA World Cup (November-December 2022). The initiative aligns with OK's stated purpose: 'making happiness tangible by planting smiles on employees, customers, suppliers, shareholders and communities.' The campaign drives foot traffic during peak festive season, clears stock, and reinforces brand positioning as customer-centric. It follows H1 2023 results showing volumes -6.3% and revenue +94.6% . Subsequent Q3 2023 performance (Dec 2022) likely benefited from this promotion.

headline: Stage set for Soccer Stars banquet
content: Delta Beverages has announced the return of the Soccer Star of the Year awards after a two-year hiatus caused by the COVID-19 pandemic which halted football in 2020 and most of 2021, with the 2022 edition scheduled for December 2, 2022 in Harare and introducing a Fans Choice Award allowing supporters to vote for their outstanding player of the season via www.castlelager.co.zw. A panel of 50 sports journalists, 18 coaches and 18 Premier Soccer League club captains last week voted the eleven Soccer Star of the Year finalists, Goalkeeper of the Year and Coach of the Year, with champions FC Platinum dominating with three players—Walter Musona (14 goals, 6 assists, favourite for Soccer Star), Wallace Magalane (16 clean sheets) and Gift Bello (seventh career league title). Other finalists include Ngezi Platinum Stars' Nelson Chadya (19 clean sheets, favourite for Goalkeeper of the Year) and Delic Murimba, CAPS United's William Manondo (17 goals, Golden Boot winner), Dynamos' Frank Makarati, Highlanders' Devine Mhindirira, ZPC Kariba's Brian Muza, Chicken Inn's Tinotenda Benza and Manica Diamonds' Elie Ilunga. FC Platinum coach Norman Mapeza leads Coach of the Year after winning the club's fourth successive league title with four Coach of the Month accolades. CAPS United's Joel Ngodzo was the last recipient of the Soccer Star of the Year accolade in 2019. This sponsorship renewal follows Delta's December 2019 confirmation of a new three-year Castle Lager PSL deal through 2022 (DLTA013) and continues the company's 125-year operating history and extensive sports development portfolio.

headline: Diminished Communications Network (DECONET): Econet fails to cast out load-shedding demon
content: Econet remains highly susceptible to perennial power outages. Power shortages are expected to further weigh on margins. ZPC announced a month-long load shedding. Econet is one of the biggest single consumers of electrical power. National grid failures necessitate resorting to costly alternatives (solar, generators, fuel). HY2023 results showed EBITDA plunged 17% due to cost pressures. Tariffs from POTRAZ are trailing behind inflation, negating recovery efforts. Inflationary pressures led to increased theft of diesel, batteries and solar panels, requiring enhanced security and further weighing on financials. The negative financial effects of power outages will be felt through lost revenue, repair/replacement of damaged equipment, and manpower costs.

headline: Who is who amongst the best dividend paying firms in 2022
content: COMPREHENSIVE ANALYSIS RANKS ZIMBABWE STOCK EXCHANGE LISTED COMPANIES BY DIVIDEND YIELD USING CLOSING SHARE PRICE ON DIVIDEND DECLARATION DATE, WITH FIRST CAPITAL BANK LEADING AT OVER 11 PERCENT YIELD FROM TWO DIVIDEND PAYMENTS AND IMPRESSIVE SHARE PRICE APPRECIATION OF OVER 350 PERCENT YTD — FOLLOWED BY FBC HOLDINGS (6.43% YIELD), OK ZIMBABWE (5.70% YIELD WITH HYBRID CURRENCY PAYOUTS TWICE DURING THE YEAR), INNSCOR AFRICA (4.48% YIELD FROM TWO DIVIDENDS AND 340 PERCENT SHARE PRICE GAIN), DELTA CORPORATION (4.38% YIELD WITH HYBRID DIVIDEND AND MAJORITY SALES IN US DOLLARS), NATIONAL FOODS (JUST UNDER 4% YIELD FROM TWO DIVIDENDS IN DIFFERENT CURRENCIES), CALEDONIA MINING CORPORATION (3.21% YIELD FROM US$0.14 QUARTERLY DIVIDENDS), AXIA CORPORATION (2.90% YIELD FROM TWO PAYMENTS), NMB LIMITED (2.65% YIELD, FIRST DIVIDEND SINCE 2019 WITH 368 PERCENT YTD SHARE PRICE GAIN), AND ZB FINANCIAL HOLDINGS (2.042% YIELD) — THE ARTICLE NOTES OVER 25 COMPANIES PAID DIVIDENDS IN 2022 INCLUDING DAIRIBOARD WHICH LATER REVERSED ITS DECLARATION, AND HIGHLIGHTS THE TREND OF HYBRID CURRENCY DIVIDENDS AMID RAGING INFLATION WHICH ERODES VALUE BETWEEN DECLARATION AND PAYMENT DATES

headline: Lager beers spur Delta volumes
content: Afdis +10% on new PET line, cider fermentation","Delta Corporation reported strong festive season performance for the third quarter ended December 31, 2022 with revenue growing 44 percent (53 percent for nine months) driven by volume growth across all segments and replacement cost-based pricing, as lager beer volume grew 17 percent for the quarter and 18 percent for nine months, sorghum beer volumes increased 11 percent (12 percent nine months) following the Chibuku brand's 60th anniversary and June 2022 launch of Chibuku Super banana flavour, and soft drinks volume rose 5 percent (14 percent nine months) despite constrained PET pack supply to be addressed by additional capacity investment expected in Q1 2023. Associate company African Distillers Limited recorded 10 percent volume growth in the third quarter and 11 percent in nine months, with cider supply constrained by regional glass bottle shortages—a challenge offset through commissioning a new PET line for packaging spirits and commencing fermentation of ciders, building on Afdis's US$1 million cider plant commissioned in Q1 2022 (AFDIS010, AFDIS017) and 4th Street localisation strategy (AFDIS014, AFDIS015, AFDIS016). Natbrew Zambia recorded 11 percent volume growth in nine months lifted by regional exports, while United National Breweries South Africa volumes remained flat for the quarter due to power cuts and reduced market service by resellers and distributors responding to fuel price increases, though grew 21 percent in nine months. Delta's local operations recorded significant volume growth in the proportion of forex sales with corresponding increase in purchases settled in foreign currency as the economy dollarised, though product supply experienced intermittent gaps from power and water rationing and demand-supply mismatches, with installation of additional packaging plant on schedule for H1 2023 commissioning. Company secretary Faith Musinga noted the economy is poised for growth driven by agriculture and mining, though financial out-turn in FY2023 may be affected by foreign currency tax assessments arising from differing interpretations of currency of payment legislation for certain taxes.

headline: Dairy sector reaps benefits from Govt-private sector close cooperation
content: MILK PRODUCTION TO REGISTER 36PC GROWTH WITH OUTPUT INCREASING FROM 75 MILLION LITRES IN 2020 TO 91.4 MILLION LITRES IN 2022, DRIVEN BY GOVERNMENT SUPPORT THROUGH THE 5 PERCENT DUTY ON DAIRY IMPORTS FUNDING THE DAIRY REVITALISATION PROGRAMME, TRAINING INITIATIVES, AND IMPROVED GENETICS — LANDS PERMANENT SECRETARY DR JOHN BASERA NOTES MILK POWDER IMPORTS DROPPED 17 PERCENT FROM 8.9 MILLION KG TO 7.4 MILLION KG, REFLECTING SUCCESSFUL IMPORT SUBSTITUTION — GWEBI AGRICULTURAL COLLEGE MAINTAINS 696 DAIRY CATTLE WITH 360 MILKING HERD PRODUCING 15 LITRES PER COW, WITH DAIRIBOARD AS THEIR MAJOR CUSTOMER — THE LIVESTOCK AND RECOVERY GROWTH PLAN TARGETS 150 MILLION LITRES ANNUALLY AND DAIRY HERD EXPANSION FROM 39,980 TO 60,000 BY 2025, REQUIRING US$75 MILLION FOR FULL SECTOR REVITALISATION

headline: Huge boost for Chiwandire, Delta Force Academy
content: Boxer Kudakwashe Chiwandire and her stable Delta Force Academy received anchor sponsorship from RM Plumbing ahead of her WBC super bantamweight title challenge against Yamileth Mercado in Mexico (March 18, 2023) [citation:article]. RM Plumbing will provide financial/material support and employment opportunities for boxers post-career. CEO Robson Muromba emphasized 'building lasting solutions that go beyond the foreseeable future' and equipping athletes for life after sport [citation:article]. Zororo-Phumulani (Doves Zimbabwe) pledged a US$3,000+ winning bonus, having previously provided flight tickets for Chiwandire's Zambia fight and a US$2,000 bonus for her title defense [citation:article]. The partnership highlights corporate social responsibility in sport, athlete welfare focus, and the 'life after sport' theme. While Delta Corporation is not directly involved, the 'Delta Force' name creates indirect brand association. The broader lesson: corporates increasingly recognizing sport as a platform for social impact and brand building."

headline: BAT tax contribution jumps 59pc to $18bn
content: BAT tax contribution jumps 59pc to $18bn","BAT Zimbabwe's tax contribution to ZIMRA surged 59% to $17.5 billion in 2022, driven by solid profit growth and price adjustments. Despite volumes dipping 6.7% to 1,054 million sticks due to RTGS shortages and competitive USD pricing, revenue jumped 50% to $24.3 billion. Gross profit grew 74% to $18.3 billion, but profit before tax increased a more modest 20% to $9 billion, impacted by exchange losses. Export volumes of cut-rag tobacco fell 43% due to reduced export demand. The company cites smart pricing mechanisms and market liquidity issues as factors. BAT expresses confidence in navigating the economic challenges through brand equity and strategy implementation to deliver shareholder value.

headline: Beautiful agriculture story that needs retelling
content: President Mnangagwa's Second Republic has driven agricultural transformation through strong leadership including Dr John Basera, former Seed Co agronomy and extension services manager, now serving as Permanent Secretary alongside Dr Anxious Jongwe Masuka. The agriculture resurgence has delivered record wheat harvest of 375,000 tonnes (surplus against 360,000 tonne national requirement), maize projections of 2.3 million tonnes (58 percent increase), private sector funding accounting for 60 percent of wheat production through contractors including Innscor's PHI and Agrowth contracting 13,000 hectares for National Foods, and horticulture exports reaching US$86.6 million (12 percent rise) with first-ever citrus exports to China. Dr Basera's appointment follows his distinguished career at Seed Co, where he excelled as agronomy and extension services manager before joining Government. This builds on Seed Co's extensive agricultural development portfolio including the Stapleford vegetable trial station (SEED004), the Grower Transformation Initiative (SEED005), and the company's role in Command Agriculture and Presidential Inputs Scheme as documented in PAC reports. No CBZ Holdings, African Distillers Limited, Delta Corporation, Econet Wireless, or any other ZSE-listed company previously documented in this corpus is mentioned, though your corpus documents Dr Basera's subsequent career trajectory as CBZ Agro-Yield Managing Director (CBZ061) and CBZ Technical Director (CBZ077) before his Government appointment, which this article omits.

headline: Economic expansion to drive BAT growth
content: Zimbabwe's anticipated economic growth (Government projection 3.8% for 2023) is expected to improve bottom-of-the-pyramid liquidity, benefiting consumer companies like BAT. Analysts IH Securities project moderate volume uplift for BAT in FY2023 driven by increased mining activity, strong agricultural production, public sector wage revisions, and China's market reopening post-COVID. However, pricing pressure from competitors and elevated dollarised costs will likely constrain margins. FY2022 saw volumes contract 6.7% to 1.05bn sticks due to inflationary pressures and ZWL liquidity crunch, with cut-rag exports plunging 43% on weak export demand. Revenue remains domestically weighted (cigarettes 97% of net revenue vs 3% exports). IH Securities forecasts US$31.85m revenue for FY23, with EBITDA margins moderating to 40% and net income of US$10.5m. Medium-term excise duties expected to revert to dollar-era rates, pressuring net revenue. Subsequent FY23 results (April 2024) showed volumes slid further (-5% to 1.003bn sticks) with cut-rag exports down 32%, though historical revenue grew 937% on inflation.

headline: CBZ Holdings' Taxation More than Doubles in FY2022
content: CBZ Holdings' FY2022 taxation charges more than doubled (+111%) to ZWL21.48 billion, reflecting an aggressive tax regime. Monetary loss widened 58% to ZWL38 billion due to currency depreciation. Interest expenses soared to ZWL13 billion. Total deposits grew to ZWL680 billion, advances to ZWL176 billion. Non-interest income surged 129% to ZWL179 billion, outpacing interest income (ZWL87bn) as banks diversify from traditional lending. After-tax profit increased to ZWL33 billion from ZWL26 billion. Total comprehensive income grew to ZWL43 billion. CEO Blessing Mudavanhu noted the operating environment remained tough despite improved performance.

headline: Econet Expands Technological Capabilities, Splashes US$66 MN on Network Upgrades
content: "Econet invested US$66 million in network expansion and upgrades in FY2023. The Group added 12 new 5G base stations (launched 2022 in partnership with Ericsson and ZTE) and commissioned 80 new base stations to meet growing demand. The company commenced deployment of a new digital Know Your Customer (KYC) platform and re-launched its enhanced Yamurai (WhatsApp Bot). Despite this, the Group incurred ZWL77 billion in exchange losses (23% of revenue) due to the ZWD shedding 85%. Chairman James Myers stated the tariff environment is a threat to long-term viability and curtails investment ability. National grid failure further pressured operating costs. Future focus will be on exploiting 4G/5G opportunities."

headline: OK Zimbabwe Modernizes Operations with ERP Implementation
content: "OK Zimbabwe delayed the publication of its FY2023 financial statements (year ended March 31, 2023), originally due June 30, 2023, due to the implementation of a new Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) System at the beginning of the financial year. The system migration resulted in delayed finalisation of financial statements. The company expected to publish results by July 31, 2023. The ERP system is expected to provide benefits including cost reduction, improved decision-making, better inventory management, enhanced financial management, real-time data analytics, and improved customer service through a 360-degree customer view. The Board and Management apologized for any inconvenience caused."

headline: Defending champion confident of exciting Econet Vic Falls Marathon
content: The 15th edition of the Econet Victoria Falls Marathon took place on July 2, 2023, attracting over 4,500 runners from 42 countries – an 11% increase from 2022 and a massive jump from 600 participants 15 years ago [citation:1][citation:6]. Defending champion Jonathan Chinyoka (who finished 7th) and other local athletes competed for US$20,000 prize money, with 42km winners receiving US$1,250 each [citation:1][citation:5]. Econet has sponsored the event since 2009 [citation:1][citation:2][citation:4]. COO Kezito Makuni (who participated) stated Econet is committed to supporting the marathon as part of the national vision of creating a US$5 billion tourism economy by 2025 [citation:1][citation:8]. The marathon delivers significant economic impact to Victoria Falls, with hotels fully booked and residents earning from BnB rentals [citation:8]. For Econet, the sponsorship builds brand equity, aligns with wellness positioning, and demonstrates commitment to national economic development.

headline: ‘Manufacturing sector confidence impressive’
content: President commissions Delta's US$70m plants, Afdis receives new line","President Emmerson Mnangagwa has commissioned three new and improved manufacturing plants worth US$70 million at Delta Beverages in Harare, comprising a soft drink PET bottling line for Coca-Cola at Graniteside to improve supply of convenience packs and regain market share, a new Chibuku Super factory to enhance traditional beer flagship brand, and a high-tech lager beer packaging line in Southerton to enable customer choice on brand and pack previously constrained by capacity. The President commended Delta for growing from strength to strength and living up to its promise of continuing to invest in Zimbabwe's economy, noting the company has invested more than US$169 million since 2017 in modernising manufacturing and bottling plants, distribution fleet, cold drink equipment and ancillary systems, demonstrating confidence by manufacturing sector players in the Second Republic's business-friendly policies. President Mnangagwa urged Delta to increase contract farming arrangements with local farmers beyond barley, sorghum and other critical ingredients to also cover maize, ensuring contracted farmers remain productive throughout the year, and expected more companies to adopt the contracting model to complement Government farming programmes. Delta Beverages managing director Max Valela confirmed the investments were delayed by COVID-19 and applauded Government for adopting pragmatic pro-business policies, adding that apart from the three projects commissioned today, the company has installed yet another line at its subsidiary Afdis . Valela stated these projects will have significant multiplier effects on the economy, with potential for all value chain partners to increase output by 30 percent for lager beer, 33 percent for soft drinks and 33 percent for sorghum beer, driving economic growth if consumer spending responds. Delta directly employs approximately 5,000 people and contributes significantly to the fiscus through excise duty, VAT, income tax and PAYE. This US$70 million investment forms part of Delta's broader US$50 million five-year capex programme announced in August 2023 which included US$1 million allocated to Afdis (CONSOLIDATED007, 2023-08-02), following Afdis's own US$1 million cider fermentation plant commissioned in Q1 2022 (AFDIS010, AFDIS017) and 4th Street localisation strategy (AFDIS014-017).

headline: Mash Holdings in US$15m property development
content: Mashonaland Holdings announced the ground-breaking of its US$15 million Pomona Commercial Centre, a 14,000 sqm development responding to the trend of office spaces moving out of the CBD [citation:article]. OK Zimbabwe will be the anchor tenant [citation:article]. Construction began July 2023 with expected completion November 2024 [citation:article]. The project is part of Mash Holdings' portfolio diversification strategy to reduce exposure to underperforming CBD office properties [citation:article]. By June 2024, the project was 57% complete and over 85% pre-leased, reflecting strong demand [citation:1]. Pomona contributed to Mash Holdings' revenue growth of 23% (US$2.62m) in early 2024 [citation:1]. The development aligns with NDS1 objectives and was praised by Minister Ziyambi Ziyambi [citation:article]. For OK Zimbabwe, the anchor tenancy expands its premium footprint in Harare's growing northern suburbs. Subsequent updates confirmed project completion by late 2024, with nearly 50% leased by September 2025 [citation:6][citation:10]

headline: Chitown hosts Chibuku Neshamwari Dance Festival
content: The 2023 Chibuku Neshamwari Traditional Dance Festival continued with its Harare provincial final at Gungwa Bar, Chikwanha (Chitungwiza) on July 15, 2023, selecting the 10th national finalist . The festival celebrates its 60th anniversary in 2023 under the theme 'Bira Remadhanzi! 60 years of Sithokozisa Ngezomgido!' . National finals are set for July 29 at Harare Gardens with prize money: US$15,000 (1st), US$10,000 (2nd), US$7,500 (3rd) . Delta Marketing Director Irimayi Muzorewa confirmed 10 provincial finals will culminate in the national event . GM Corporate Affairs Patricia Murambinda invited Chitungwiza residents to 'enjoy and celebrate dance and music' . The festival, which debuted at Mai Musodzi Hall in Mbare, has run for six decades in partnership with NACZ and ZNTDA . 2022 winners Dapurahunanzva (Manicaland, Mbakumba dance) took 20 years to win, demonstrating the festival's competitive depth . For Delta, this represents deep cultural engagement and brand heritage

headline: Econet Unveils New Offerings"
content: "Econet is implementing a virtualized core network using network function virtualization (NFV) to increase flexibility, scalability, and cost-effectiveness. This replaces dedicated hardware with virtualized software on general-purpose servers, allowing easier introduction of new services (VPNs, cloud services, SDN). The company has also begun deploying a new digital Know Your Customer (KYC) platform to streamline customer onboarding, using advanced data analytics and identity verification. The article notes that Econet's services have been poor in urban and rural areas, and suggests the company should increase network penetration in rural areas, partner with names like Starlink, and continue investing in fibre optics, 5G, cloud computing, IoT, and AI to improve customer service and extend revenue streams. A feasibility study shows customers are tapping into NetOne due to poor Econet services in many areas.

headline: ‘Seed Co ready for agricultural season, El Nino’
content: Seed Co Limited, the country's largest seed producer, reported total seed sales volumes growth of 10 percent for the first quarter ended June 30, 2023, with wheat sales constituting 85 percent of overall volume driven by a 7 percent surge in wheat seed sales and 19 percent increase in barley seed sales attributable to enhanced dam water levels and improved electricity availability for irrigation, according to company secretary Tineyi Chatiza in a trading update. The group's production and processing plants have been strategically planned to accumulate an ideal mix of seed stock varieties to cater to preliminary El Nino forecast scenarios of below-normal to normal rainfall. Revenue grew 648 percent historically and 305 percent in inflation-adjusted terms, consistent with sharp depreciation of the exchange rate and consequent inflationary effects. Operating profit showed a positive swing of 10 times when compared to previous period's inflation-adjusted loss and 19 times compared to historical profit, attributed to recovery of profit margins and alignment of the exchange rate with open market forces. The seed giant continues investing more in drought-tolerant maize seed varieties through a breeding programme for developing early-maturing seed varieties to counter the effects of climate change, which is making droughts more frequent and severe globally, threatening the staple maize crop. This follows Zimbabwe's 2020 launch of a US$47 million 7-year project with Green Climate Fund and UNDP support to strengthen climate resilience of vulnerable communities, with programmes such as Pfumvudza smart agriculture increasing resilience against climate change-induced drought impacts. Subsequent developments revealed the challenging impact of the El Niño forecast: by December 2023, Seed Co Limited's maize sales volumes had decreased 22 percent from the prior year with group CEO Morgan Nzwere noting the environment remains uncertain and 'the biggest issue is the El Niño effect that Zimbabwe in particular is facing,' with sales volumes at about 50 percent of budget and farmers delaying purchases pending rain expectations, while regional associate Seed Co International benefited from rains in East Africa with stockouts in Zambia, Tanzania and Kenya and encouraging performance in Nigeria, Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania and Zambia .

headline: OK Zimbabwe Further Delays Results Amid ERP Transition"
content: "OK Zimbabwe again deferred its FY2023 results, originally due March 31, 2023, then postponed to June 30, 2023, with a new target date of August 31, 2023. The continued delay is attributed to the transition to a new ERP system at the start of the financial year. The company faces potential fines or suspension under ZSE listing rules (Section 39(1)) requiring publication within three months of year-end. The article explains that ERP implementation is complex, requiring significant time to migrate and reconcile data, and can lead to changes in accounting policies. Technical issues or data inconsistencies can further prolong the process. The system offers long-term advantages once fully implemented.

headline: Delta to invest US$50m into capex programmes
content: "Delta to invest US$50m into capex programmes; Afdis receives US$1m for capacity expansion","Delta Corporation plans to invest US$50 million over the next five years to boost production capacity, spending between 30 and 35 percent of operating profit on plant, equipment and ancillary services, having already invested more than US$169 million since 2017 in modernising manufacturing and bottling plants, distribution fleet, cold drink equipment and ancillary systems, including the recent commissioning of three new production lines worth US$71 million. The new plant investments are split into lager beers US$15 million, Chibuku Super US$20 million, soft drinks PET Line US$10 million, vehicle fleet US$5 million, containers US15 million and US$1 million in its associate company, Afdis . The new lager plant, manufactured by Germany's Krones, runs four bottle types at capacities up to 42,000 bottles per hour (700 per minute) for 375ml/340ml and 25,000 bottles per hour (417 per minute) for 750ml/660ml, delivering daily production of 350,000 litres and raising national beer packaging capacity from 2.2 million hectolitres to 2.9 million hectolitres annually. The soft drinks PET line produces 700,000 hectolitres (70 million litres) per annum across 350ml, 500ml and two-litre formats, increasing sparkling business unit capacity utilisation to 47 percent, while the new Chibuku line fills 20,000 bottles per hour (250 hectolitres) with annual output of 1.3 million hectolitres. Delta confirmed a farmer-centric approach targeting 100 percent of its 11,500 direct farmers—including both commercial-scale and smallholder farmers—to be skilled, connected and financially empowered by 2025, having achieved a 92:8 contracting split (by tonnage) between communal and commercial sectors for 2022 sorghum production and contracting 120 commercial farmers for barley. The company requires 35,000 tonnes of barley, 20,000 tonnes sorghum grain, 60,000 tonnes of maize and 20,000 tonnes of sugar annually. This capex programme and farmer empowerment strategy aligns with Delta's broader US$40-50 million annual investment guidance  and follows Afdis's own US$1 million cider fermentation plant commissioned in Q1 2022 (AFDIS010, AFDIS017) and 4th Street localisation (AFDIS014, AFDIS015, AFDIS016)."
